vanity fair by william makepeace thackeray before the curtain as the manager of the performance sit before the curtain on the board and look into the fair a feeling of profound melancholy come over he in his survey of the bustling place
there be a great quantity of eating and drink make love and jilt laugh and the contrary smoking cheating fighting dancing and fiddle there be bully push about buck ogle the woman knave pick pocket policeman on the look-out quack other quack plague take they bawl in front of their booth and yokel look up at the tinsel dancer and poor old rouge tumbler while the light-finger folk be operate upon their pocket behind
yes this be vanity fair not a moral place certainly nor a merry one though very noisy
look at the face of the actor and buffoon when they come off from their business and tom fool wash the paint off his cheek before he sit down to dinner with his wife and the little jack puddings behind the canvas
the curtain will be up presently and he will be turn over head and heel and cry how be you a man with a reflective turn of mind walk through an exhibition of this sort will not be oppress i take it by his own or other people s hilarity
an episode of humour or kindness touch and amuse he here and there--a pretty child look at a gingerbread stall a pretty girl blushing whilst her lover talk to she and choose her fair poor tom fool yonder behind the waggon mumble his bone with the honest family which live by his tumbling but the general impression be one more melancholy than mirthful
when you come home you sit down in a sober contemplative not uncharitable frame of mind and apply yourself to your book or your business
i have no other moral than this to tag to the present story of vanity fair some people consider fairs immoral altogether and eschew such with their servant and family very likely they be right
but person who think otherwise and be of a lazy or a benevolent or a sarcastic mood may perhaps like to step in for half an hour and look at the performance
there be scene of all sort some dreadful combat some grand and lofty horse-riding some scene of high life and some of very middling indeed some love-making for the sentimental and some light comic business the whole accompany by appropriate scenery and brilliantly illuminate with the author s own candle
what more have the manager of the performance to say to acknowledge the kindness with which it have be receive in all the principal town of england through which the show have pass and where it have be most favourably notice by the respected conductor of the public press and by the nobility and gentry
he be proud to think that his puppet have give satisfaction to the very good company in this empire
the famous little becky puppet have be pronounce to be uncommonly flexible in the joint and lively on the wire the amelia doll though it have have a small circle of admirer have yet be carve and dress with the great care by the artist the dobbin figure though apparently clumsy yet dance in a very amusing and natural manner the little boys dance have be like by some and please to remark the richly dress figure of the wicked nobleman on which no expense have be spar and which old nick will fetch away at the end of this singular performance
and with this and a profound bow to his patron the manager retire and the curtain rise

wig at the rate of four mile an hour
a black servant who repose on the box beside the fat coachman uncurl his bandy leg as soon as the equipage draw up opposite miss pinkerton s shin brass plate and as he pull the bell at least a score of young head be see peer out of the narrow window of the stately old brick house

only when her pupil quit the establishment or when they be about to be marry and once when poor miss birch die of the scarlet fever be miss pinkerton know to write personally to the parent of her pupil and it be jemima s opinion that if anything could console mrs
birch for her daughter s loss it would be that pious and eloquent composition in which miss pinkerton announce the event
in the present instance miss pinkerton s billet be to the follow effect the mall chiswick june 15 18 madam after her six year residence at the mall i have the honour and happiness of present miss amelia sedley to her parent as a young lady not unworthy to occupy a fitting position in their polished and refined circle
those virtue which characterize the young english gentlewoman those accomplishment which become her birth and station will not be find want in the amiable miss sedley whose industry and obedience have endear she to her instructor and whose delightful sweetness of temper have charm her aged and her youthful companion
in music in dancing in orthography in every variety of embroidery and needlework she will be find to have realize her friend fond wish
in geography there be still much to be desire and a careful and undeviating use of the backboard for four hour daily during the next three year be recommend as necessary to the acquirement of that dignified deportment and carriage so requisite for every young lady of fashion
in the principle of religion and morality miss sedley will be find worthy of an establishment which have be honour by the presence of the great lexicographer and the patronage of the admirable mrs
chapone
in leave the mall miss amelia carry with she the heart of her companion and the affectionate regard of her mistress who have the honour to subscribe herself madam your most obliged humble servant barbara pinkerton p s miss sharp accompanie miss sedley
it be particularly request that miss sharp s stay in russell square may not exceed ten day
the family of distinction with whom she be engage desire to avail themselves of her service as soon as possible
this letter complete miss pinkerton proceed to write her own name and miss sedley s in the fly-leaf of a johnson s dictionary--the interesting work which she invariably present to her scholar on their departure from the mall
on the cover be insert a copy of line address to a young lady on quit miss pinkerton s school at the mall by the late revere doctor samuel johnson in fact the lexicographer s name be always on the lip of this majestic woman and a visit he have pay to she be the cause of her reputation and her fortune
be command by her eld sister to get the dictionary from the cupboard miss jemima have extract two copy of the book from the receptacle in question

for becky sharp answer jemima tremble very much and blush over her withered face and neck as she turn she back on her sister

and so venture not to say another word poor jemima trot off exceedingly flurried and nervous
miss sedley s papa be a merchant in london and a man of some wealth whereas miss sharp be an articled pupil for whom miss pinkerton have do as she think quite enough without confer upon she at part the high honour of the dixonary
although schoolmistress letter be to be trust no more nor less than churchyard epitaph yet as it sometimes happen that a person depart this life who be really deserve of all the praise the stone cutter carve over his bone who be a good christian a good parent child wife or husband who actually do leave a disconsolate family to mourn his loss so in academy of the male and female sex it occur every now and then that the pupil be fully worthy of the praise bestow by the disinterested instructor
now miss amelia sedley be a young lady of this singular specie and deserve not only all that miss pinkerton say in her praise but have many charming quality which that pompous old minerva of a woman could not see from the difference of rank and age between her pupil and herself
for she could not only sing like a lark or a mrs
billington and dance like hillisberg or parisot and embroider beautifully and spell as well as a dixonary itself but she have such a kindly smile tender gentle generous heart of her own as win the love of everybody who come near she from minerva herself down to the poor girl in the scullery and the one-eyed tart-woman s daughter who be permit to vend her ware once a week to the young lady in the mall
she have twelve intimate and bosom friend out of the twenty-four young lady
even envious miss briggs never speak ill of she high and mighty miss saltire lord dexter s granddaughter allow that her figure be genteel and as for miss swartz the rich woolly-haired mulatto from st
kitt s on the day amelia go away she be in such a passion of tear that they be oblige to send for dr
floss and half tipsify she with salvolatile

kitt s
such luxury of grief however be only allow to parlour-boarder
honest jemima have all the bill and the washing and the mending and the pudding and the plate and crockery and the servant to superintend
but why speak about she
it be probable that we shall not hear of she again from this moment to the end of time and that when the great filigree iron gate be once close on she she and her awful sister will never issue therefrom into this little world of history
but as we be to see a great deal of amelia there be no harm in say at the outset of our acquaintance that she be a dear little creature and a great mercy it be both in life and in novel which and the latter especially abound in villain of the most sombre sort that we be to have for a constant companion so guileless and good-natured a person
as she be not a heroine there be no need to describe her person indeed i be afraid that her nose be rather short than otherwise and her cheek a great deal too round and red for a heroine but her face blush with rosy health and her lip with the fresh of smile and she have a pair of eye which sparkle with the bright and honest good-humour except indeed when they fill with tear and that be a great deal too often for the silly thing would cry over a dead canary-bird or over a mouse that the cat haply have seize upon or over the end of a novel be it ever so stupid and as for say an unkind word to she be any person hard-hearted enough to do so--why so much the bad for they
even miss pinkerton that austere and godlike woman cease scold she after the first time and though she no more comprehend sensibility than she do algebra give all master and teacher particular order to treat miss sedley with the utmost gentleness as harsh treatment be injurious to she
so that when the day of departure come between her two custom of laugh and cry miss sedley be greatly puzzle how to act
she be glad to go home and yet most woefully sad at leave school
for three day before little laura martin the orphan follow she about like a little dog
she have to make and receive at least fourteen present--to make fourteen solemn promise of writing every week send my letter under cover to my grandpapa the earl of dexter say miss saltire who by the way be rather shabby
never mind the postage but write every day you dear darling say the impetuous and woolly-head but generous and affectionate miss swartz and the orphan little laura martin who be just in round-hand take she friend s hand and say look up in her face wistfully amelia when i write to you i shall call you mamma all which detail i have no doubt jones who read this book at his club will pronounce to be excessively foolish trivial twaddling and ultra-sentimental
yes i can see jones at this minute rather flushed with his joint of mutton and half pint of wine take out his pencil and score under the word foolish twaddling c and add to they his own remark of quite true well he be a lofty man of genius and admire the great and heroic in life and novel and so have well take warning and go elsewhere
well then
the flower and the present and the trunk and bonnet-box of miss sedley have be arrange by mr
sambo in the carriage together with a very small and weather-beat old cow s-skin trunk with miss sharp s card neatly nail upon it which be deliver by sambo with a grin and pack by the coachman with a correspond sneer--the hour for parting come and the grief of that moment be considerably lessen by the admirable discourse which miss pinkerton address to her pupil
not that the parting speech cause amelia to philosophise or that it arm she in any way with a calmness the result of argument but it be intolerably dull pompous and tedious and have the fear of her schoolmistress greatly before her eye miss sedley do not venture in her presence to give way to any ebullition of private grief
a seed-cake and a bottle of wine be produce in the drawing-room as on the solemn occasion of the visit of parent and these refreshment be partake of miss sedley be at liberty to depart
youwill go in and say good-by to miss pinkerton becky say miss jemima to a young lady of whom nobody take any notice and who be come downstairs with her own bandbox
i suppose i must say miss sharp calmly and much to the wonder of miss jemima and the latter have knock at the door and receive permission to come in miss sharp advance in a very unconcerned manner and say in french and with a perfect accent mademoiselle je vien vous faire me adieux miss pinkerton do not understand french she only direct those who do but bite her lip and throw up her venerable and roman-nosed head on the top of which figure a large and solemn turban she say miss sharp i wish you a good morning as the hammersmith semiramis speak she wave one hand both by way of adieu and to give miss sharp an opportunity of shake one of the finger of the hand which be leave out for that purpose
miss sharp only fold her own hand with a very frigid smile and bow and quite decline to accept the proffered honour on which semiramis toss up her turban more indignantly than ever
in fact it be a little battle between the young lady and the old one and the latter be worst
heaven bless you my child say she embrace amelia and scowl the while over the girl s shoulder at miss sharp
come away becky say miss jemima pull the young woman away in great alarm and the drawing-room door close upon they for ever

chapter ii in which miss sharp and miss sedley prepare to open the campaign when miss sharp have perform the heroical act mention in the last chapter and have see the dixonary fly over the pavement of the little garden fall at length at the foot of the astonish miss jemima the young lady s countenance which have before wear an almost livid look of hatred assume a smile that perhaps be scarcely more agreeable and she sink back in the carriage in an easy frame of mind saying-- so much for the dixonary and thank god ibe out of chiswick miss sedley be almost as flurry at the act of defiance as miss jemima have be for consider it be but one minute that she have leave school and the impression of six year be not get over in that space of time
nay with some person those awe and terror of youth last for ever and ever
i know for instance an old gentleman of sixty-eight who say to i one morning at breakfast with a very agitated countenance i dream last night that i be flog by dr
raine fancy have carry he back five-and-fifty year in the course of that evening
dr
raine and his rod be just as awful to he in his heart then at sixty-eight as they have be at thirteen
if the doctor with a large birch have appear bodily to he even at the age of threescore and eight and have say in awful voice boy take down your pant--
well well miss sedley be exceedingly alarmed at this act of insubordination
how could you do so rebecca at last she say after a pause
why do you think miss pinkerton will come out and order i back to the black-hole say rebecca laugh
no but-- i hate the whole house continue miss sharp in a fury

i wish it be in the bottom of the thames i do and if miss pinkerton be there i wouldnot pick she out that i wouldnot
o how i should like to see she float in the water yonder turban and all with her train streaming after she and her nose like the beak of a wherry hush cry miss sedley
why will the black footman tell tale cry miss rebecca laugh
he may go back and tell miss pinkerton that i hate she with all my soul and i wish he would and i wish i have a means of prove it too
for two year i have only have insult and outrage from she
i have be treat bad than any servant in the kitchen
i have never have a friend or a kind word except from you
i have be make to tend the little girl in the low schoolroom and to talk french to the misses until i grow sick of my mother tongue
but that talk french to miss pinkerton be capital fun benot it
she donot know a word of french and be too proud to confess it
i believe it be that which make her part with i and so thank heaven for french

vive bonaparte o rebecca rebecca for shame cry miss sedley for this be the great blasphemy rebecca have as yet utter and in those day in england to say long live bonaparte be as much as to say long live lucifer how can you--how dare you have such wicked revengeful thought revenge may be wicked but itbe natural answer miss rebecca
ibe no angel and to say the truth she certainly be not
for it may be remark in the course of this little conversation which take place as the coach roll along lazily by the river side that though miss rebecca sharp have twice have occasion to thank heaven it have be in the first place for rid she of some person whom she hat and secondly for enable she to bring her enemy to some sort of perplexity or confusion neither of which be very amiable motif for religious gratitude or such as would be put forward by person of a kind and placable disposition
miss rebecca be not then in the least kind or placable
all the world use her ill say this young misanthropist and we may be pretty certain that person whom all the world treat ill deserve entirely the treatment they get
the world be a look-glass and give back to every man the reflection of his own face
frown at it and it will in turn look sourly upon you laugh at it and with it and it be a jolly kind companion and so let all young person take their choice
this be certain that if the world neglect miss sharp she never be know to have do a good action in behalf of anybody nor can it be expect that twenty-four young lady should all be as amiable as the heroine of this work miss sedley whom we have select for the very reason that she be the well-natured of all otherwise what on earth be to have prevent we from put up miss swartz or miss crump or miss hopkins as heroine in her place it could not be expect that every one should be of the humble and gentle temper of miss amelia sedley should take every opportunity to vanquish rebecca s hard-heartedness and ill-humour and by a thousand kind word and office overcome for once at least her hostility to her kind
miss sharp s father be an artist and in that quality have give lesson of draw at miss pinkerton s school
he be a clever man a pleasant companion a careless student with a great propensity for run into debt and a partiality for the tavern
when he be drunk he use to beat his wife and daughter and the next morning with a headache he would rail at the world for its neglect of his genius and abuse with a good deal of cleverness and sometimes with perfect reason the fool his brother painter
as it be with the utmost difficulty that he could keep himself and as he owe money for a mile round soho where he live he think to better his circumstance by marry a young woman of the french nation who be by profession an opera-girl
the humble calling of her female parent miss sharp never allude to but use to state subsequently that the entrechats be a noble family of gascony and take great pride in her descent from they
and curious it be that as she advance in life this young lady s ancestor increase in rank and splendour
rebecca s mother have have some education somewhere and her daughter speak french with purity and a parisian accent
it be in those day rather a rare accomplishment and lead to her engagement with the orthodox miss pinkerton
for her mother be dead her father find himself not likely to recover after his third attack of delirium tremen write a manly and pathetic letter to miss pinkerton recommend the orphan child to her protection and so descend to the grave after two bailiff have quarrel over his corpse
rebecca be seventeen when she come to chiswick and be bind over as an article pupil her duty be to talk french as we have see and her privilege to live cost free and with a few guineas a year to gather scrap of knowledge from the professor who attend the school
she be small and slight in person pale sandy-haired and with eye habitually cast down when they look up they be very large odd and attractive so attractive that the reverend mr
crisp fresh from oxford and curate to the vicar of chiswick the reverend mr
flowerdew fall in love with miss sharp be shoot dead by a glance of her eye which be fire all the way across chiswick church from the school-pew to the reading-desk
this infatuated young man use sometimes to take tea with miss pinkerton to whom he have be present by his mamma and actually propose something like marriage in an intercepted note which the one-eyed apple-woman be charge to deliver
mrs
crisp be summon from buxton and abruptly carry off her darling boy but the idea even of such an eagle in the chiswick dovecot cause a great flutter in the breast of miss pinkerton who would have send away miss sharp but that she be bind to she under a forfeit and who never could thoroughly believe the young lady s protestation that she have never exchange a single word with mr
crisp except under her own eye on the two occasion when she have meet he at tea
by the side of many tall and bounce young lady in the establishment rebecca sharp look like a child
but she have the dismal precocity of poverty
many a dun have she talk to and turn away from her father s door many a tradesman have she coax and wheedle into good-humour and into the granting of one meal more
she sate commonly with her father who be very proud of her wit and hear the talk of many of his wild companion--often but ill-suited for a girl to hear
but she never have be a girl she say she have be a woman since she be eight year old
oh why do miss pinkerton let such a dangerous bird into her cage
the fact be the old lady believe rebecca to be the meek creature in the world so admirably on the occasion when her father bring she to chiswick use rebecca to perform the part of the ingenue and only a year before the arrangement by which rebecca have be admit into her house and when rebecca be sixteen year old miss pinkerton majestically and with a little speech make she a present of a doll--which be by the way the confiscate property of miss swindle discover surreptitiously nurse it in school-hour
how the father and daughter laugh as they trudge home together after the evening party it be on the occasion of the speech when all the professor be invite and how miss pinkerton would have rag have she see the caricature of herself which the little mimic rebecca manage to make out of her doll
becky use to go through dialogue with it it form the delight of newman street gerrard street and the artist quarter and the young painter when they come to take their gin-and-water with their lazy dissolute clever jovial senior use regularly to ask rebecca if miss pinkerton be at home she be as well known to they poor soul
as mr
lawrence or president west
once rebecca have the honour to pass a few day at chiswick after which she bring back jemima and erect another doll as miss jemmy for though that honest creature have make and give her jelly and cake enough for three child and a seven-shilling piece at parting the girl s sense of ridicule be far strong than her gratitude and she sacrifice miss jemmy quite as pitilessly as her sister
the catastrophe come and she be bring to the mall as to her home
the rigid formality of the place suffocate she the prayer and the meal the lesson and the walk which be arrange with a conventual regularity oppress she almost beyond endurance and she look back to the freedom and the beggary of the old studio in soho with so much regret that everybody herself include fancy she be consume with grief for her father
she have a little room in the garret where the maid hear she walk and sob at night but it be with rage and not with grief
she have not be much of a dissembler until now her loneliness teach she to feign
she have never mingle in the society of woman her father reprobate as he be be a man of talent his conversation be a thousand time more agreeable to she than the talk of such of her own sex as she now encounter
the pompous vanity of the old schoolmistress the foolish good-humour of her sister the silly chat and scandal of the eld girl and the frigid correctness of the governess equally annoy she and she have no soft maternal heart this unlucky girl otherwise the prattle and talk of the young child with whose care she be chiefly intrust might have soothe and interested she but she live among they two year and not one be sorry that she go away
the gentle tender-hearted amelia sedley be the only person to whom she could attach herself in the least and who could help attach herself to amelia
the happiness the superior advantage of the young woman round about she give rebecca inexpressible pang of envy
what air that girl give herself because she be an earl s grand-daughter she say of one
how they cringe and bow to that creole because of her hundred thousand pound
i be a thousand time cleverer and more charming than that creature for all her wealth
i be as well breed as the earl s grand-daughter for all her fine pedigree and yet every one pass i by here
and yet when i be at my father s do not the man give up their gay ball and party in order to pass the evening with i she determine at any rate to get free from the prison in which she find herself and now begin to act for herself and for the first time to make connected plan for the future
she take advantage therefore of the mean of study the place offer she and as she be already a musician and a good linguist she speedily go through the little course of study which be consider necessary for lady in those day
her music she practise incessantly and one day when the girl be out and she have remain at home she be overhear to play a piece so well that minerva thought wisely she could spare herself the expense of a master for the junior and intimate to miss sharp that she be to instruct they in music for the future
the girl refuse and for the first time and to the astonishment of the majestic mistress of the school
i be here to speak french with the child rebecca say abruptly not to teach they music and save money for you
give i money and i will teach they minerva be oblige to yield and of course dislike she from that day
for five-and-thirty year she say and with great justice i never have see the individual who have dare in my own house to question my authority
i have nourish a viper in my bosom a viper--a fiddlestick say miss sharp to the old lady almost faint with astonishment
you take i because i be useful
there be no question of gratitude between we
i hate this place and want to leave it
i will do nothing here but what i be oblige to do it be in vain that the old lady ask she if she be aware she be speak to miss pinkerton
rebecca laugh in her face with a horrid sarcastic demoniacal laughter that almost send the schoolmistress into fit
give i a sum of money say the girl and get rid of i--or if you like well get i a good place as governess in a nobleman s family--you can do so if you please and in their further dispute she always return to this point get i a situation--we hate each other and i be ready to go worthy miss pinkerton although she have a roman nose and a turban and be as tall as a grenadier and have be up to this time an irresistible princess have no will or strength like that of her little apprentice and in vain do battle against she and try to overawe she
attempt once to scold she in public rebecca hit upon the before-mention plan of answer she in french which quite rout the old woman
in order to maintain authority in her school it become necessary to remove this rebel this monster this serpent this firebrand and hear about this time that sir pitt crawley s family be in want of a governess she actually recommend miss sharp for the situation firebrand and serpent as she be

boggley wollah be situate in a fine lonely marshy jungly district famous for snipe-shooting and where not unfrequently you may flush a tiger
ramgunge where there be a magistrate be only forty mile off and there be a cavalry station about thirty mile far so joseph write home to his parent when he take possession of his collectorship

he do not live with his family while in london but have lodging of his own like a gay young bachelor

if people would but leave child to themselves if teacher would cease to bully they if parent would not insist upon direct their thought and dominate their feeling--those feeling and thought which be a mystery to all for how much do you and i know of each other of our child of our father of our neighbour and how far more beautiful and sacred be the thought of the poor lad or girl whom you govern likely to be than those of the dull and world-corrupt person who rule him if i say parent and master would leave their child alone a little more small harm would accrue although a less quantity of as in praesenti might be acquire

chapter vi vauxhall i know that the tune i be pip be a very mild one although there be some terrific chapter come presently and must beg the good-natured reader to remember that we be only discourse at present about a stockbroker s family in russell square who be take walk or luncheon or dinner or talk and make love as people do in common life and without a single passionate and wonderful incident to mark the progress of their love
the argument stand thus--osborne in love with amelia have ask an old friend to dinner and to vauxhall--jos sedley be in love with rebecca
will he marry she
that be the great subject now in hand
we might have treat this subject in the genteel or in the romantic or in the facetious manner
suppose we have lay the scene in grosvenor square with the very same adventure--would not some people have listen
suppose we have show how lord joseph sedley fall in love and the marquis of osborne become attach to lady amelia with the full consent of the duke her noble father or instead of the supremely genteel suppose we have resort to the entirely low and describe what be go on in mr
sedley s kitchen--how black sambo be in love with the cook as indeed he be and how he fight a battle with the coachman in her behalf how the knife-boy be catch steal a cold shoulder of mutton and miss sedley s new femme de chambre refuse to go to bed without a wax candle such incident might be make to provoke much delightful laughter and be suppose to represent scene of life or if on the contrary we have take a fancy for the terrible and make the lover of the new femme de chambre a professional burglar who burst into the house with his band slaughter black sambo at the foot of his master and carry off amelia in her night-dress not to be let loose again till the third volume we should easily have construct a tale of thrilling interest through the fiery chapter of which the reader should hurry pant
but my reader must hope for no such romance only a homely story and must be content with a chapter about vauxhall which be so short that it scarce deserve to be call a chapter at all

chapter vii crawley of queen s crawley among the most respected of the name begin in c which the court-guide contain in the year 18 be that of crawley sir pitt baronet great gaunt street and queen s crawley hant
this honourable name have figure constantly also in the parliamentary list for many year in conjunction with that of a number of other worthy gentleman who sit in turn for the borough
it be relate with regard to the borough of queen s crawley that queen elizabeth in one of her progress stop at crawley to breakfast be so delighted with some remarkably fine hampshire beer which be then present to she by the crawley of the day a handsome gentleman with a trim beard and a good leg that she forthwith erect crawley into a borough to send two member to parliament and the place from the day of that illustrious visit take the name of queen s crawley which it hold up to the present moment
and though by the lapse of time and those mutation which age produce in empire city and borough queen s crawley be no long so populous a place as it have be in queen bess s time--nay be come down to that condition of borough which use to be denominate rotten--yet as sir pitt crawley would say with perfect justice in his elegant way rotten
be hang--it produce i a good fifteen hundred a year sir pitt crawley name after the great commoner be the son of walpole crawley first baronet of the tape and sealing-wax office in the reign of george ii when he be impeach for peculation as be a great number of other honest gentleman of those day and walpole crawley be as need scarcely be say son of john churchill crawley name after the celebrated military commander of the reign of queen anne
the family tree which hang up at queen s crawley furthermore mention charles stuart afterwards call barebones crawley son of the crawley of james the first s time and finally queen elizabeth s crawley who be represent as the foreground of the picture in his fork beard and armour

at four o clock on such a roseate summer s morning as even make great gaunt street look cheerful the faithful tinker have waken she bedfellow and bid her prepare for departure unbarred and unbolt the great hall door the clanging and clapping whereof startle the sleeping echo in the street and take her way into oxford street summon a coach from a stand there
it be needless to particularize the number of the vehicle or to state that the driver be station thus early in the neighbourhood of swallow street in hope that some young buck reel homeward from the tavern might need the aid of his vehicle and pay he with the generosity of intoxication
it be likewise needless to say that the driver if he have any such hope as those above state be grossly disappoint and that the worthy baronet whom he drive to the city do not give he one single penny more than his fare
it be in vain that jehu appeal and storm that he fling down miss sharp s bandbox in the gutter at the neck and swear he would take the law of his fare
youwould well not say one of the ostler itbe sir pitt crawley so it be joe cry the baronet approvingly and iwould like to see the man can do i so should oi say joe grin sulkily and mount the baronet s baggage on the roof of the coach
keep the box for i leader exclaim the member of parliament to the coachman who reply yes sir pitt with a touch of his hat and rage in his soul for he have promise the box to a young gentleman from cambridge who would have give a crown to a certainty and miss sharp be accommodate with a back seat inside the carriage which might be say to be carry she into the wide world
how the young man from cambridge sulkily put his five great-coat in front but be reconcile when little miss sharp be make to quit the carriage and mount up beside he--when he cover she up in one of his benjamins and become perfectly good-humour--how the asthmatic gentleman the prim lady who declare upon her sacred honour she have never travel in a public carriage before there be always such a lady in a coach--alas
be for the coach where be they and the fat widow with the brandy-bottle take their place inside--how the porter ask they all for money and get sixpence from the gentleman and five greasy halfpence from the fat widow--and how the carriage at length drive away--now thread the dark lane of aldersgate anon clattering by the blue cupola of st
paul s jingle rapidly by the stranger entry of fleet-market which with exeter change have now depart to the world of shadow--how they pass the white bear in piccadilly and see the dew rise up from the market-garden of knightsbridge--how turnhamgreen brentwood bagshot be pass--need not be tell here
but the writer of these page who have pursue in former day and in the same bright weather the same remarkable journey cannot but think of it with a sweet and tender regret
where be the road now and its merry incident of life
be there no chelsea or greenwich for the old honest pimple-nosed coachman
i wonder where be they those good fellow
be old weller alive or dead
and the waiter yea and the inn at which they wait and the cold round of beef inside and the stunted ostler with his blue nose and clink pail where be he and where be his generation
to those great genius now in petticoat who shall write novel for the beloved reader s child these man and thing will be as much legend and history as nineveh or coeur de lion or jack sheppard
for they stage-coach will have become romance--a team of four bay as fabulous as bucephalus or black bess
ah how their coat shine as the stable-man pull their clothe off and away they go--ah how their tail shake as with smoking side at the stage s end they demurely walk away into the inn-yard
alas
we shall never hear the horn sing at midnight or see the pike-gate fly open any more
whither however be the light four-inside trafalgar coach carry we
let we be set down at queen s crawley without further divagation and see how miss rebecca sharp speed there
chapter viii private and confidential miss rebecca sharp to miss amelia sedley russell square london
free pitt crawley my dearest sweetest amelia with what mingle joy and sorrow do i take up the pen to write to my dear friend
oh what a change between to-day and yesterday
now i be friendless and alone yesterday i be at home in the sweet company of a sister whom i shall ever ever cherish
i will not tell you in what tear and sadness i pass the fatal night in which i separate from you
you go on tuesday to joy and happiness with your mother and your devoted young soldier by your side and i think of you all night dance at the perkins s the prettiest i be sure of all the young lady at the ball
i be bring by the groom in the old carriage to sir pitt crawley s town house where after john the groom have behave most rudely and insolently to i alas
twa safe to insult poverty and misfortune i be give over to sir p s care and make to pass the night in an old gloomy bed and by the side of a horrid gloomy old charwoman who keep the house
i do not sleep one single wink the whole night
sir pitt be not what we silly girl when we use to read cecilia at chiswick imagine a baronet must have be
anything indeed less like lord orville cannot be imagine
fancy an old stumpy short vulgar and very dirty man in old clothe and shabby old gaiter who smoke a horrid pipe and cook his own horrid supper in a saucepan
he speak with a country accent and swear a great deal at the old charwoman at the hackney coachman who drive we to the inn where the coach go from and on which i make the journey outside for the greater part of the way
i be awaken at daybreak by the charwoman and have arrive at the inn be at first place inside the coach
but when we get to a place call leakington where the rain begin to fall very heavily--will you believe it i be force to come outside for sir pitt be a proprietor of the coach and as a passenger come at mudbury who want an inside place i be oblige to go outside in the rain where however a young gentleman from cambridge college shelter i very kindly in one of his several great coat
this gentleman and the guard seem to know sir pitt very well and laugh at he a great deal
they both agree in call he an old screw which mean a very stingy avaricious person
he never give any money to anybody they say and this meanness i hate and the young gentleman make i remark that we drive very slow for the last two stage on the road because sir pitt be on the box and because he be proprietor of the horse for this part of the journey
but willnot i flog them on to squashmore when i take the ribbon say the young cantab
and sarve them right master jack say the guard
when i comprehend the meaning of this phrase and that master jack intend to drive the rest of the way and revenge himself on sir pitt s horse of course i laugh too
a carriage and four splendid horse cover with armorial bearing however await we at mudbury four mile from queen s crawley and we make our entrance to the baronet s park in state
there be a fine avenue of a mile long lead to the house and the woman at the lodge-gate over the pillar of which be a serpent and a dove the supporter of the crawley arm make we a number of curtsy as she fling open the old iron carve door which be something like those at odious chiswick
therebe an avenue say sir pitt a mile long
therebe six thousand pound of timber in they there tree
do you call that nothing he pronounce avenue--evenue and nothing--nothink so droll and he have a mr
hodson his hind from mudbury into the carriage with he and they talk about distraining and sell up and drain and subsoiling and a great deal about tenant and farming--much more than i could understand
sam miles have be catch poach and peter bailey have go to the workhouse at last
serve he right say sir pitt he and his family have be cheat i on that farm these hundred and fifty year some old tenant i suppose who could not pay his rent
sir pitt might have say he and his family to be sure but rich baronet do not need to be careful about grammar as poor governess must be
as we pass i remark a beautiful church-spire rise above some old elm in the park and before they in the midst of a lawn and some outhouse an old red house with tall chimney cover with ivy and the window shin in the sun
be that your church sir i say
yes hang it say sir pitt only he use dear a much wickeder word howbe buty hodson
buty s my brother bute my dear--my brother the parson
buty and the beast i call he ha ha hodson laugh too and then look more grave and nod his head say ibe afraid hebe well sir pitt
he be out on his pony yesterday look at our corn look after his tithe hang un only he use the same wicked word
will brandy and water never kill he
hebe as tough as old whatdyecallum--old methusalem mr
hodson laugh again
the young man be home from college
theyhave whop john scroggins till hebe well nigh dead whop my second keeper roar out sir pitt
he be on the parson s ground sir reply mr

however he say ihave sell the presentation of the living hodson none of that breed shall get it i war nt and mr
hodson say he be quite right and i have no doubt from this that the two brother be at variance--as brother often be and sister too
donot you remember the two miss scratchleys at chiswick how they use always to fight and quarrel--and mary box how she be always thump louisa
presently see two little boy gather stick in the wood mr

haw haw before the house of queen s crawley which be an odious old-fashion red brick mansion with tall chimney and gable of the style of queen bess there be a terrace flank by the family dove and serpent and on which the great hall-door open

i think there be at least twenty bedroom on the first floor one of they have the bed in which queen elizabeth sleep and i have be take by my new pupil through all these fine apartment this morning
they be not render less gloomy i promise you by have the shutter always shut and there be scarce one of the apartment but when the light be let into it i expect to see a ghost in the room

ever and ever thine own rebecca everything consider i think it be quite as well for our dear amelia sedley in russell square that miss sharp and she be part
rebecca be a droll funny creature to be sure and those description of the poor lady weep for the loss of her beauty and the gentleman with hay-colour whisker and straw-colour hair be very smart doubtless and show a great knowledge of the world
that she might when on her knee have be think of something well than miss horrock s ribbon have possibly strike both of we
but my kind reader will please to remember that this history have vanity fair for a title and that vanity fair be a very vain wicked foolish place full of all sort of humbug and falseness and pretension
and while the moralist who be hold forth on the cover an accurate portrait of your humble servant profess to wear neither gown nor band but only the very same long-eared livery in which his congregation be array yet look you one be bind to speak the truth as far as one know it whether one mount a cap and bell or a shovel hat and a deal of disagreeable matter must come out in the course of such an undertaking
i have hear a brother of the story-tell trade at naples preach to a pack of good-for-nothing honest lazy fellow by the sea-shore work himself up into such a rage and passion with some of the villain whose wicked deed he be describe and invent that the audience could not resist it and they and the poet together would burst out into a roar of oath and execration against the fictitious monster of the tale so that the hat go round and the bajocchi tumble into it in the midst of a perfect storm of sympathy
at the little paris theatre on the other hand you will not only hear the people yell out ah gredin
ah monstre and curse the tyrant of the play from the box but the actor themselves positively refuse to play the wicked part such as those of infame anglais brutal cossack and what not and prefer to appear at a small salary in their real character as loyal frenchmen
i set the two story one against the other so that you may see that it be not from mere mercenary motif that the present performer be desirous to show up and trounce his villain but because he have a sincere hatred of they which he cannot keep down and which must find a vent in suitable abuse and bad language
i warn my kyind friend then that i be go to tell a story of harrowing villainy and complicated--but as i trust intensely interesting--crime
my rascal be no milk-and-water rascal i promise you
when we come to the proper place we willnot spare fine language--no no
but when we be go over the quiet country we must perforce be calm
a tempest in a slop-basin be absurd
we will reserve that sort of thing for the mighty ocean and the lonely midnight
the present chapter be very mild
other--but we will not anticipate those
and as we bring our character forward i will ask leave as a man and a brother not only to introduce they but occasionally to step down from the platform and talk about they if they be good and kindly to love they and shake they by the hand if they be silly to laugh at they confidentially in the reader s sleeve if they be wicked and heartless to abuse they in the strong term which politeness admit of
otherwise you might fancy it be i who be sneer at the practice of devotion which miss sharp find so ridiculous that it be i who laugh good-humouredly at the reeling old silenus of a baronet--whereas the laughter come from one who have no reverence except for prosperity and no eye for anything beyond success
such people there be live and flourish in the world--faithless hopeless charityless let we have at they dear friend with might and main
some there be and very successful too mere quack and fool and it be to combat and expose such as those no doubt that laughter be make
chapter ix family portraits sir pitt crawley be a philosopher with a taste for what be call low life
his first marriage with the daughter of the noble binkie have be make under the auspex of his parent and as he often tell lady crawley in her lifetime she be such a confound quarrelsome high-breed jade that when she die he be hang if he would ever take another of her sort at her ladyship s demise he keep his promise and select for a second wife miss rose dawson daughter of mr
john thomas dawson ironmonger of mudbury
what a happy woman be rose to be my lady crawley
let we set down the item of her happiness
in the first place she give up peter butt a young man who keep company with she and in consequence of his disappointment in love take to smuggling poach and a thousand other bad course
then she quarrel as in duty bind with all the friend and intimate of her youth who of course could not be receive by my lady at queen s crawley--nor do she find in her new rank and abode any person who be willing to welcome she
who ever do
sir huddleston fuddleston have three daughter who all hop to be lady crawley
sir giles wapshot s family be insult that one of the wapshot girl have not the preference in the marriage and the remain baronet of the county be indignant at their comrade s misalliance
never mind the commoner whom we will leave to grumble anonymously
sir pitt do not care as he say a brass farden for any one of they
he have his pretty rose and what more need a man require than to please himself
so he use to get drunk every night to beat his pretty rose sometimes to leave she in hampshire when he go to london for the parliamentary session without a single friend in the wide world
even mrs
bute crawley the rector s wife refuse to visit she as she say she would never give the pas to a tradesman s daughter
as the only endowment with which nature have gift lady crawley be those of pink cheek and a white skin and as she have no sort of character nor talent nor opinion nor occupation nor amusement nor that vigour of soul and ferocity of temper which often fall to the lot of entirely foolish woman her hold upon sir pitt s affection be not very great
her rose fade out of her cheek and the pretty freshness leave her figure after the birth of a couple of child and she become a mere machine in her husband s house of no more use than the late lady crawley s grand piano
be a light-complexion woman she wear light clothe as most blonde will and appear in preference in draggle sea-green or slatternly sky-blue
she work that worst day and night or other piece like it
she have counterpane in the course of a few year to all the bed in crawley
she have a small flower-garden for which she have rather an affection but beyond this no other like or dislike
when her husband be rude to she she be apathetic whenever he strike she she cry
she have not character enough to take to drinking and moan about slipshod and in curl-paper all day
o vanity fair--vanity fair
this might have be but for you a cheery lass--peter butt and rose a happy man and wife in a snug farm with a hearty family and an honest portion of pleasure care hope and struggle--but a title and a coach and four be toy more precious than happiness in vanity fair and if harry the eighth or bluebeard be alive now and want a tenth wife do you suppose he could not get the prettiest girl that shall be present this season
the languid dulness of their mamma do not as it may be suppose awaken much affection in her little daughter but they be very happy in the servant hall and in the stable and the scotch gardener have luckily a good wife and some good child they get a little wholesome society and instruction in his lodge which be the only education bestow upon they until miss sharp come
her engagement be owe to the remonstrance of mr
pitt crawley the only friend or protector lady crawley ever have and the only person besides her child for whom she entertain a little feeble attachment
mr
pitt take after the noble binkies from whom he be descend and be a very polite and proper gentleman
when he grow to man s estate and come back from christchurch he begin to reform the slacken discipline of the hall in spite of his father who stand in awe of he
he be a man of such rigid refinement that he would have starve rather than have din without a white neckcloth
once when just from college and when horrock the butler bring he a letter without place it previously on a tray he give that domestic a look and administer to he a speech so cutting that horrock ever after tremble before he the whole household bow to he lady crawley s curl-paper come off early when he be at home sir pitt s muddy gaiter disappear and if that incorrigible old man still adhere to other old habit he never fuddle himself with rum-and-water in his son s presence and only talk to his servant in a very reserved and polite manner and those person remark that sir pitt never swear at lady crawley while his son be in the room
it be he who teach the butler to say my lady be serve and who insist on hand her ladyship in to dinner
he seldom speak to she but when he do it be with the most powerful respect and he never let she quit the apartment without rise in the most stately manner to open the door and make an elegant bow at her egress
at eton he be call miss crawley and there i be sorry to say his young brother rawdon use to lick he violently
but though his part be not brilliant he make up for his lack of talent by meritorious industry and be never know during eight year at school to be subject to that punishment which it be generally think none but a cherub can escape
at college his career be of course highly creditable
and here he prepare himself for public life into which he be to be introduce by the patronage of his grandfather lord binkie by study the ancient and modern orator with great assiduity and by speak unceasingly at the debating society
but though he have a fine flux of word and deliver his little voice with great pomposity and pleasure to himself and never advance any sentiment or opinion which be not perfectly trite and stale and support by a latin quotation yet he fail somehow in spite of a mediocrity which ought to have insure any man a success
he do not even get the prize poem which all his friend say he be sure of
after leave college he become private secretary to lord binkie and be then appoint attache to the legation at pumpernickel which post he fill with perfect honour and bring home despatch consist of strasburg pie to the foreign minister of the day
after remain ten year attache several year after the lamented lord binkie s demise and find the advancement slow he at length give up the diplomatic service in some disgust and begin to turn country gentleman
he write a pamphlet on malt on return to england for he be an ambitious man and always like to be before the public and take a strong part in the negro emancipation question
then he become a friend of mr
wilberforce s whose politic he admire and have that famous correspondence with the reverend silas hornblower on the ashantee mission
he be in london if not for the parliament session at least in may for the religious meeting
in the country he be a magistrate and an active visitor and speaker among those destitute of religious instruction
he be say to be pay his address to lady jane sheepshanks lord southdown s third daughter and whose sister lady emily write those sweet tract the sailor s true binnacle and the applewoman of finchley common miss sharp s account of his employment at queen s crawley be not caricature
he subject the servant there to the devotional exercise before mention in which and so much the well he bring his father to join
he patronise an independent meeting-house in crawley parish much to the indignation of his uncle the rector and to the consequent delight of sir pitt who be induce to go himself once or twice which occasion some violent sermon at crawley parish church direct point-blank at the baronet s old gothic pew there
honest sir pitt however do not feel the force of these discourse as he always take his nap during sermon-time
mr
crawley be very earnest for the good of the nation and of the christian world that the old gentleman should yield he up his place in parliament but this the elder constantly refuse to do
both be of course too prudent to give up the fifteen hundred a year which be bring in by the second seat at this period fill by mr
quadroon with carte blanche on the slave question indeed the family estate be much embarrassed and the income draw from the borough be of great use to the house of queen s crawley
it have never recover the heavy fine impose upon walpole crawley first baronet for peculation in the tape and sealing wax office
sir walpole be a jolly fellow eager to seize and to spend money alieni appeten sui profusus as mr
crawley would remark with a sigh and in his day belove by all the county for the constant drunkenness and hospitality which be maintain at queen s crawley
the cellar be fill with burgundy then the kennel with hound and the stable with gallant hunter now such horse as queen s crawley possess go to plough or run in the trafalgar coach and it be with a team of these very horse on an off-day that miss sharp be bring to the hall for boor as he be sir pitt be a stickler for his dignity while at home and seldom drive out but with four horse and though he din off boil mutton have always three footman to serve it
if mere parsimony could have make a man rich sir pitt crawley might have become very wealthy--if he have be an attorney in a country town with no capital but his brain it be very possible that he would have turn they to good account and might have achieve for himself a very considerable influence and competency
but he be unluckily endow with a good name and a large though encumbered estate both of which go rather to injure than to advance he
he have a taste for law which cost he many thousand yearly and be a great deal too clever to be rob as he say by any single agent allow his affair to be mismanage by a dozen whom he all equally mistrust
he be such a sharp landlord that he could hardly find any but bankrupt tenant and such a close farmer as to grudge almost the seed to the ground whereupon revengeful nature grudge he the crop which she grant to more liberal husbandman
he speculate in every possible way he work mine buy canal-share horse coach take government contract and be the busy man and magistrate of his county
as he would not pay honest agent at his granite quarry he have the satisfaction of find that four overseer run away and take fortune with they to america
for want of proper precaution his coal-mine fill with water the government fling his contract of damaged beef upon his hand and for his coach-horse every mail proprietor in the kingdom know that he lose more horse than any man in the country from underfeede and buy cheap
in disposition he be sociable and far from be proud nay he rather prefer the society of a farmer or a horse-dealer to that of a gentleman like my lord his son he be fond of drink of swearing of joke with the farmer daughter he be never know to give away a shilling or to do a good action but be of a pleasant sly laugh mood and would cut his joke and drink his glass with a tenant and sell he up the next day or have his laugh with the poacher he be transport with equal good humour
his politeness for the fair sex have already be hint at by miss rebecca sharp--in a word the whole baronetage peerage commonage of england do not contain a more cunning mean selfish foolish disreputable old man
that blood-red hand of sir pitt crawley s would be in anybody s pocket except his own and it be with grief and pain that as admirer of the british aristocracy we find ourselves oblige to admit the existence of so many ill quality in a person whose name be in debrett
one great cause why mr
crawley have such a hold over the affection of his father result from money arrangement
the baronet owe his son a sum of money out of the jointure of his mother which he do not find it convenient to pay indeed he have an almost invincible repugnance to pay anybody and could only be bring by force to discharge his debt
miss sharp calculate for she become as we shall hear speedily induct into most of the secret of the family that the mere payment of his creditor cost the honourable baronet several hundred yearly but this be a delight he could not forego he have a savage pleasure in make the poor wretch wait and in shift from court to court and from term to term the period of satisfaction
whatbe the good of be in parliament he say if you must pay your debt
hence indeed his position as a senator be not a little useful to he
vanity fair--vanity fair
here be a man who could not spell and do not care to read--who have the habit and the cunning of a boor whose aim in life be pettifog who never have a taste or emotion or enjoyment but what be sordid and foul and yet he have rank and honours and power somehow and be a dignitary of the land and a pillar of the state
he be high sheriff and ride in a golden coach
great minister and statesman court he and in vanity fair he have a high place than the most brilliant genius or spotless virtue
sir pitt have an unmarried half-sister who inherit her mother s large fortune and though the baronet propose to borrow this money of she on mortgage miss crawley decline the offer and prefer the security of the fund
she have signify however her intention of leave she inheritance between sir pitt s second son and the family at the rectory and have once or twice pay the debt of rawdon crawley in his career at college and in the army
miss crawley be in consequence an object of great respect when she come to queen s crawley for she have a balance at her banker s which would have make she beloved anywhere
what a dignity it give an old lady that balance at the banker s
how tenderly we look at her fault if she be a relative and may every reader have a score of such what a kind good-natured old creature we find she
how the junior partner of hobbs and dobbs lead her smile to the carriage with the lozenge upon it and the fat wheezy coachman
how when she come to pay we a visit we generally find an opportunity to let our friend know her station in the world
we say and with perfect truth i wish i have miss macwhirter s signature to a cheque for five thousand pound
she wouldnot miss it say your wife
she be my aunt say you in an easy careless way when your friend ask if miss macwhirter be any relative
your wife be perpetually send she little testimony of affection your little girl work endless worst basket cushion and footstool for she
what a good fire there be in her room when she come to pay you a visit although your wife lace her stay without one
the house during her stay assume a festive neat warm jovial snug appearance not visible at other season
you yourself dear sir forget to go to sleep after dinner and find yourself all of a sudden though you invariably lose very fond of a rubber
what good dinner you have--game every day malmsey-madeira and no end of fish from london
even the servant in the kitchen share in the general prosperity and somehow during the stay of miss macwhirter s fat coachman the beer be grow much strong and the consumption of tea and sugar in the nursery where she maid take her meal be not regard in the least
be it so or be it not so

chapter x miss sharp begin to make friend and now be receive as a member of the amiable family whose portrait we have sketch in the forego page it become naturally rebecca s duty to make herself as she say agreeable to her benefactor and to gain their confidence to the utmost of her power
who can but admire this quality of gratitude in an unprotected orphan and if there enter some degree of selfishness into her calculation who can say but that her prudence be perfectly justifiable
i be alone in the world say the friendless girl
i have nothing to look for but what my own labour can bring i and while that little pink-face chit amelia with not half my sense have ten thousand pound and an establishment secure poor rebecca and my figure be far well than her have only herself and her own wit to trust to
well let we see if my wit cannot provide i with an honourable maintenance and if some day or the other i cannot show miss amelia my real superiority over she
not that i dislike poor amelia who can dislike such a harmless good-natured creature only it will be a fine day when i can take my place above she in the world as why indeed should i not thus it be that our little romantic friend form vision of the future for herself--nor must we be scandalise that in all her castle in the air a husband be the principal inhabitant
of what else have young lady to think but husband
of what else do their dear mamma think
i must be my own mamma say rebecca not without a tingle consciousness of defeat as she think over her little misadventure with jos sedley
so she wisely determine to render her position with the queen s crawley family comfortable and secure and to this end resolve to make friend of every one around she who could at all interfere with her comfort
as my lady crawley be not one of these personage and a woman moreover so indolent and void of character as not to be of the least consequence in her own house rebecca soon find that it be not at all necessary to cultivate her good will--indeed impossible to gain it
she use to talk to her pupil about their poor mamma and though she treat that lady with every demonstration of cool respect it be to the rest of the family that she wisely direct the chief part of her attention
with the young people whose applause she thoroughly gain her method be pretty simple
she do not pester their young brain with too much learning but on the contrary let they have their own way in regard to educate themselves for what instruction be more effectual than self-instruction
the eld be rather fond of book and as there be in the old library at queen s crawley a considerable provision of work of light literature of the last century both in the french and english language they have be purchase by the secretary of the tape and sealing wax office at the period of his disgrace and as nobody ever trouble the bookshelf but herself rebecca be enable agreeably and as it be in play to impart a great deal of instruction to miss rose crawley
she and miss rose thus read together many delightful french and english work among which may be mention those of the learn dr
smollett of the ingenious mr
henry fielding of the graceful and fantastic monsieur crebillon the young whom our immortal poet gray so much admire and of the universal monsieur de voltaire

crawley ask what the young people be read the governess reply smollett oh smollett say mr
crawley quite satisfied
his history be more dull but by no mean so dangerous as that of mr
hume

humphrey clinker

mr
crawley as a diplomatist be exceedingly proud of his own skill in speak the french language for he be of the world still and not a little pleased with the compliment which the governess continually pay he upon his proficiency
miss violet s taste be on the contrary more rude and boisterous than those of her sister
she know the sequester spot where the hen lay their egg
she could climb a tree to rob the nest of the feather songster of their speckled spoil
and her pleasure be to ride the young colt and to scour the plain like camilla
she be the favourite of her father and of the stableman
she be the darling and withal the terror of the cook for she discover the haunt of the jam-pot and would attack they when they be within her reach
she and her sister be engage in constant battle
any of which peccadillo if miss sharp discover she do not tell they to lady crawley who would have tell they to the father or bad to mr
crawley but promise not to tell if miss violet would be a good girl and love her governess
with mr
crawley miss sharp be respectful and obedient
she use to consult he on passage of french which she could not understand though her mother be a frenchwoman and which he would construe to her satisfaction and besides give she his aid in profane literature he be kind enough to select for her book of a more serious tendency and address to her much of his conversation
she admire beyond measure his speech at the quashimaboo-aid society take an interest in his pamphlet on malt be often affect even to tear by his discourse of an evening and would say-- oh thank you sir with a sigh and a look up to heaven that make he occasionally condescend to shake hand with she
blood be everything after all would that aristocratic religionist say
how miss sharp be awaken by my word when not one of the people here be touch

her mother be a montmorency indeed it be from this famous family as it appear that miss sharp by the mother s side be descend
of course she do not say that her mother have be on the stage it would have shock mr

be we to suppose from this curiosity and pry into dictionary could our heroine suppose that mr

have we not state that he be attach to lady jane sheepshanks
he take rebecca to task once or twice about the propriety of play at backgammon with sir pitt say that it be a godless amusement and that she would be much well engage in read thrump s legacy or the blind washerwoman of moorfields or any work of a more serious nature but miss sharp say her dear mother use often to play the same game with the old count de trictrac and the venerable abbe du cornet and so find an excuse for this and other worldly amusement
but it be not only by play at backgammon with the baronet that the little governess render herself agreeable to her employer
she find many different way of be useful to he
she read over with indefatigable patience all those law paper with which before she come to queen s crawley he have promise to entertain she
she volunteer to copy many of his letter and adroitly alter the spelling of they so as to suit the usage of the present day
she become interested in everything appertain to the estate to the farm the park the garden and the stable and so delightful a companion be she that the baronet would seldom take his after-breakfast walk without she and the child of course when she would give her advice as to the tree which be to be lop in the shrubbery the garden-bed to be dig the crop which be to be cut the horse which be to go to cart or plough
before she have be a year at queen s crawley she have quite win the baronet s confidence and the conversation at the dinner-table which before use to be hold between he and mr
horrocks the butler be now almost exclusively between sir pitt and miss sharp
she be almost mistress of the house when mr
crawley be absent but conduct herself in her new and exalt situation with such circumspection and modesty as not to offend the authority of the kitchen and stable among whom her behaviour be always exceedingly modest and affable
she be quite a different person from the haughty shy dissatisfied little girl whom we have know previously and this change of temper prove great prudence a sincere desire of amendment or at any rate great moral courage on her part
whether it be the heart which dictate this new system of complaisance and humility adopt by our rebecca be to be prove by she after-history
a system of hypocrisy which last through whole year be one seldom satisfactorily practise by a person of one-and-twenty however our reader will recollect that though young in year our heroine be old in life and experience and we have write to no purpose if they have not discover that she be a very clever woman
the elder and young son of the house of crawley be like the gentleman and lady in the weather-box never at home together--they hat each other cordially indeed rawdon crawley the dragoon have a great contempt for the establishment altogether and seldom come thither except when his aunt pay her annual visit
the great good quality of this old lady have be mention
she possess seventy thousand pound and have almost adopt rawdon
she dislike her eld nephew exceedingly and despise he as a milksop
in return he do not hesitate to state that her soul be irretrievably lose and be of opinion that his brother s chance in the next world be not a whit well
she be a godless woman of the world would mr
crawley say she live with atheist and frenchmen
my mind shudder when i think of her awful awful situation and that near as she be to the grave she should be so give up to vanity licentiousness profaneness and folly in fact the old lady decline altogether to hear his hour s lecture of an evening and when she come to queen s crawley alone he be oblige to pretermit his usual devotional exercise

old miss crawley be certainly one of the reprobate
she have a snug little house in park lane and as she eat and drink a great deal too much during the season in london she go to harrowgate or cheltenham for the summer
she be the most hospitable and jovial of old vestal and have be a beauty in her day she say

it be needless to say that sir pitt be bring to change his view after the death of the great whig statesman
this worthy old lady take a fancy to rawdon crawley when a boy send he to cambridge in opposition to his brother at oxford and when the young man be request by the authority of the first-name university to quit after a residence of two year she buy he his commission in the life guards green
a perfect and celebrated blood or dandy about town be this young officer
boxing rat-hunting the five court and four-in-hand driving be then the fashion of our british aristocracy and he be an adept in all these noble science
and though he belong to the household troop who as it be their duty to rally round the prince regent have not show their valour in foreign service yet rawdon crawley have already apropo of play of which he be immoderately fond fight three bloody duel in which he give ample proof of his contempt for death
and for what follow after death would mr
crawley observe throw his gooseberry-colour eye up to the ceiling
he be always think of his brother s soul or of the soul of those who differ with he in opinion it be a sort of comfort which many of the serious give themselves
silly romantic miss crawley far from be horrify at the courage of her favourite always use to pay his debt after his duel and would not listen to a word that be whisper against his morality
he will sow his wild oat she would say and be worth far more than that pule hypocrite of a brother of his chapter xi arcadian simplicity besides these honest folk at the hall whose simplicity and sweet rural purity surely show the advantage of a country life over a town one we must introduce the reader to their relative and neighbour at the rectory bute crawley and his wife
the reverend bute crawley be a tall stately jolly shovel-hat man far more popular in his county than the baronet his brother
at college he pull stroke-oar in the christchurch boat and have thrash all the good bruiser of the town he carry his taste for box and athletic exercise into private life there be not a fight within twenty mile at which he be not present nor a race nor a course match nor a regatta nor a ball nor an election nor a visitation dinner nor indeed a good dinner in the whole county but he find mean to attend it
you might see his bay mare and gig-lamp a score of mile away from his rectory house whenever there be any dinner-party at fuddleston or at roxby or at wapshot hall or at the great lord of the county with all of whom he be intimate
he have a fine voice sing a southerly wind and a cloudy sky and give the whoop in chorus with general applause
he ride to hound in a pepper-and-salt frock and be one of the good fisherman in the county
mrs
crawley the rector s wife be a smart little body who write this worthy divine s sermon
be of a domestic turn and keep the house a great deal with her daughter she rule absolutely within the rectory wisely give her husband full liberty without
he be welcome to come and go and dine abroad as many day as his fancy dictate for mrs
crawley be a save woman and know the price of port wine
ever since mrs
bute carry off the young rector of queen s crawley she be of a good family daughter of the late lieut colonel hector mctavish and she and her mother play for bute and win he at harrowgate she have be a prudent and thrifty wife to he
in spite of her care however he be always in debt
it take he at least ten year to pay off his college bill contract during his father s lifetime
in the year 179 when he be just clear of these incumbrance he give the odd of 100 to 1 in twenty against kangaroo who win the derby
the rector be oblige to take up the money at a ruinous interest and have be struggle ever since
his sister help he with a hundred now and then but of course his great hope be in her death--when hang it as he would say matilda must leave i half her money so that the baronet and his brother have every reason which two brother possibly can have for be by the ear

rawdon it be know be to come in for the bulk of miss crawley s property
these money transaction--these speculation in life and death--these silent battle for reversionary spoil--make brother very love towards each other in vanity fair
i for my part have know a five-pound note to interpose and knock up a half century s attachment between two brother and cannot but admire as i think what a fine and durable thing love be among worldly people
it cannot be suppose that the arrival of such a personage as rebecca at queen s crawley and her gradual establishment in the good grace of all people there could be unremarke by mrs
bute crawley
mrs
bute who know how many day the sirloin of beef last at the hall how much linen be get ready at the great wash how many peach be on the south wall how many dos her ladyship take when she be ill--for such point be matter of intense interest to certain person in the country--mrs
bute i say could not pass over the hall governess without make every inquiry respect her history and character
there be always the good understanding between the servant at the rectory and the hall
there be always a good glass of ale in the kitchen of the former place for the hall people whose ordinary drink be very small--and indeed the rector s lady know exactly how much malt go to every barrel of hall beer--tie of relationship exist between the hall and rectory domestic as between their master and through these channel each family be perfectly well acquainted with the doing of the other
that by the way may be set down as a general remark
when you and your brother be friend his doing be indifferent to you
when you have quarrel all his outgoing and incoming you know as if you be his spy

dear madam i have the honour to acknowledge your polite communication to which i promptly reply

bute crawley my excellent pupil of former year the sprightly and accomplish miss martha mactavish
i be happy to have under my charge now the daughter of many of those who be your contemporary at my establishment--what pleasure it would give i if your own beloved young lady have need of my instructive superintendence
present my respectful compliment to lady fuddleston i have the honour epistolarily to introduce to her ladyship my two friend miss tuffin and miss hawky
either of these young lady be perfectly qualified to instruct in greek latin and the rudiment of hebrew in mathematic and history in spanish french italian and geography in music vocal and instrumental in dancing without the aid of a master and in the element of natural science
in the use of the globe both be proficient

the miss sharp whom you mention as governess to sir pitt crawley bart m p be a pupil of mine and i have nothing to say in her disfavour

my dread be lest the principle of the mother--who be represent to i as a french countess force to emigrate in the late revolutionary horror but who as i have since find be a person of the very low order and moral--should at any time prove to be hereditary in the unhappy young woman whom i take as an outcast

miss rebecca sharp to miss amelia sedley
