chapter xii quite a sentimental chapter we must now take leave of arcadia and those amiable people practise the rural virtue there and travel back to london to inquire what have become of miss amelia we donot care a fig for she write some unknown correspondent with a pretty little handwriting and a pink seal to her note
she be fade and insipid and add some more kind remark in this strain which i should never have repeat at all but that they be in truth prodigiously complimentary to the young lady whom they concern
have the beloved reader in his experience of society never hear similar remark by good-natured female friend who always wonder what you can see in miss smith that be so fascinating or what could induce major jones to propose for that silly insignificant simper miss thompson who have nothing but her wax-doll face to recommend she
what be there in a pair of pink cheek and blue eye forsooth
these dear moralists ask and hint wisely that the gift of genius the accomplishment of the mind the mastery of mangnall s questions and a ladylike knowledge of botany and geology the knack of make poetry the power of rattle sonata in the herz-manner and so forth be far more valuable endowment for a female than those fugitive charm which a few year will inevitably tarnish
it be quite edify to hear woman speculate upon the worthlessness and the duration of beauty
but though virtue be a much fine thing and those hapless creature who suffer under the misfortune of good look ought to be continually put in mind of the fate which await they and though very likely the heroic female character which lady admire be a more glorious and beautiful object than the kind fresh smiling artless tender little domestic goddess whom man be incline to worship--yet the latter and inferior sort of woman must have this consolation--that the man do admire they after all and that in spite of all our kind friend warning and protest we go on in our desperate error and folly and shall to the end of the chapter

the occurrence of the previous day the admirable conduct of rebecca in refuse an offer so advantageous to she the secret unhappiness preying upon she the sweetness and silence with which she bear her affliction make miss crawley much more tender than usual
an event of this nature a marriage or a refusal or a proposal thrill through a whole household of woman and set all their hysterical sympathy at work
as an observer of human nature i regularly frequent st
george s hanover square during the genteel marriage season and though i have never see the bridegroom s male friend give way to tear or the beadle and officiating clergy any way affected yet it be not at all uncommon to see woman who be not in the least concerned in the operation go on--old lady who be long past marrying stout middle-aged female with plenty of son and daughter let alone pretty young creature in pink bonnet who be on their promotion and may naturally take an interest in the ceremony--i say it be quite common to see the woman present piping sobbing sniffle hide their little face in their little useless pocket-handkerchief and heaving old and young with emotion
when my friend the fashionable john pimlico marry the lovely lady belgravia green parker the excitement be so general that even the little snuffy old pew-opener who let i into the seat be in tear
and wherefore
i inquire of my own soul she be not go to be marry
miss crawley and briggs in a word after the affair of sir pitt indulge in the utmost luxury of sentiment and rebecca become an object of the most tender interest to they

chapter xix miss crawley at nurse we have see how mrs
firkin the lady s maid as soon as any event of importance to the crawley family come to her knowledge feel bind to communicate it to mrs
bute crawley at the rectory and have before mention how particularly kind and attentive that good-natured lady be to miss crawley s confidential servant
she have be a gracious friend to miss briggs the companion also and have secure the latter s good-will by a number of those attention and promise which cost so little in the making and be yet so valuable and agreeable to the recipient
indeed every good economist and manager of a household must know how cheap and yet how amiable these profession be and what a flavour they give to the most homely dish in life
who be the blunder idiot who say that fine word butter no parsnip
half the parsnip of society be serve and render palatable with no other sauce
as the immortal alexis soyer can make more delicious soup for a half-penny than an ignorant cook can concoct with pound of vegetable and meat so a skilful artist will make a few simple and pleasing phrase go far than ever so much substantial benefit-stock in the hand of a mere bungler
nay we know that substantial benefit often sicken some stomach whereas most will digest any amount of fine word and be always eager for more of the same food
mrs
bute have tell briggs and firkin so often of the depth of her affection for they and what she would do if she have miss crawley s fortune for friend so excellent and attach that the lady in question have the deep regard for she and feel as much gratitude and confidence as if mrs
bute have load they with the most expensive favour
rawdon crawley on the other hand like a selfish heavy dragoon as he be never take the least trouble to conciliate his aunt s aide-de-camp show his contempt for the pair with entire frankness--make firkin pull off his boot on one occasion--send she out in the rain on ignominious message--and if he give she a guinea fling it to she as if it be a box on the ear
as his aunt too make a butt of briggs the captain follow the example and level his joke at she--joke about as delicate as a kick from his charger
whereas mrs
bute consult she in matter of taste or difficulty admire her poetry and by a thousand act of kindness and politeness show she appreciation of briggs and if she make firkin a twopenny-halfpenny present accompany it with so many compliment that the twopence-half-penny be transmute into gold in the heart of the grateful waiting-maid who besides be look forwards quite contentedly to some prodigious benefit which must happen to she on the day when mrs
bute come into her fortune
the different conduct of these two people be point out respectfully to the attention of person commence the world
praise everybody i say to such never be squeamish but speak out your compliment both point-blank in a man s face and behind his back when you know there be a reasonable chance of his hear it again
never lose a chance of say a kind word
as collingwood never see a vacant place in his estate but he take an acorn out of his pocket and pop it in so deal with your compliment through life
an acorn cost nothing but it may sprout into a prodigious bit of timber

bowls s plate

bute s beady eye eagerly fix on she as the latter sate steadfast in the arm-chair by the bedside

bute s twinkling eye or the flick of yellow that the rushlight throw on the dreary darken ceiling
hygeia herself would have fall sick under such a regimen and how much more this poor old nervous victim
it have be say that when she be in health and good spirit this venerable inhabitant of vanity fair have as free notion about religion and moral as monsieur de voltaire himself could desire but when illness overtake she it be aggravate by the most dreadful terror of death and an utter cowardice take possession of the prostrate old sinner
sick-bed homily and pious reflection be to be sure out of place in mere story-book and we be not go after the fashion of some novelist of the present day to cajole the public into a sermon when it be only a comedy that the reader pay his money to witness
but without preach the truth may surely be bear in mind that the bustle and triumph and laughter and gaiety which vanity fair exhibit in public do not always pursue the performer into private life and that the most dreary depression of spirit and dismal repentance sometimes overcome he
recollection of the well ordain banquet will scarcely cheer sick epicure
reminiscence of the most become dress and brilliant ball triumph will go very little way to console fade beauty
perhaps statesman at a particular period of existence be not much gratified at think over the most triumphant division and the success or the pleasure of yesterday become of very small account when a certain albeit uncertain morrow be in view about which all of we must some day or other be speculate

chapter xxiii captain dobbin proceeds on his canvass what be the secret mesmerism which friendship possess and under the operation of which a person ordinarily sluggish or cold or timid become wise active and resolute in another s behalf
as alexis after a few pass from dr

and what cause the doctor when ail to send for his rival and not sit down and examine his own tongue in the chimney bass or write his own prescription at his study-table
i throw out these query for intelligent reader to answer who know at once how credulous we be and how sceptical how soft and how obstinate how firm for other and how diffident about ourselves meanwhile it be certain that our friend william dobbin who be personally of so comply a disposition that if his parent have press he much it be probable he would have step down into the kitchen and marry the cook and who to far his own interest would have find the most insuperable difficulty in walk across the street find himself as busy and eager in the conduct of george osborne s affair as the most selfish tactician could be in the pursuit of his own

no member of the household child or domestic ever enter that room without a certain terror

george as a boy have be horsewhip in this room many time his mother sit sick on the stair listen to the cut of the whip

osborne s death--george be on a pony the eld sister hold he up a bunch of flower the young lead by her mother s hand all with red cheek and large red mouth simper on each other in the approve family-portrait manner

here this young fellow who though only seventeen year of age and about sixty-five inch high with a constitution naturally rickety and much impair by premature brandy and water have an undoubted courage and a lion s heart poise try bent and balance a weapon such as he think would do execution amongst frenchmen

our history be destine in this chapter to go backwards and forwards in a very irresolute manner seemingly and have conduct our story to to-morrow presently we shall immediately again have occasion to step back to yesterday so that the whole of the tale may get a hearing
as you behold at her majesty s drawing-room the ambassador and high dignitary carriage whisk off from a private door while captain jones s lady be wait for their fly as you see in the secretary of the treasury s antechamber a half-dozen of petitioner wait patiently for their audience and call out one by one when suddenly an irish member or some eminent personage enter the apartment and instantly walk into mr
under-secretary over the head of all the people present so in the conduct of a tale the romancer be oblige to exercise this most partial sort of justice
although all the little incident must be hear yet they must be put off when the great event make their appearance and surely such a circumstance as that which bring dobbin to brighton viz the ordering out of the guards and the line to belgium and the mustering of the ally army in that country under the command of his grace the duke of wellington--such a dignified circumstance as that i say be entitle to the pas over all minor occurrence whereof this history be compose mainly and hence a little trifling disarrangement and disorder be excusable and become

sedley who ask news about his son-in-law and about jos s carriage and whether his horse have be down to brighton and about that infernal traitor bonaparty and the war until the irish maid-servant come with a plate and a bottle of wine from which the old gentleman insist upon help the valet
he give he a half-guinea too which the servant pocket with a mixture of wonder and contempt
to the health of your master and mistress trotter mr
sedley say and herebe something to drink your health when you get home trotter there be but nine day past since amelia have leave that little cottage and home--and yet how far off the time seem since she have bid it farewell
what a gulf lie between she and that past life
she could look back to it from her present standing-place and contemplate almost as another being the young unmarried girl absorb in her love have no eye but for one special object receive parental affection if not ungratefully at least indifferently and as if it be her due--her whole heart and thought bent on the accomplishment of one desire
the review of those day so lately go yet so far away touch she with shame and the aspect of the kind parent fill she with tender remorse
be the prize gain--the heaven of life--and the winner still doubtful and unsatisfied
as his hero and heroine pass the matrimonial barrier the novelist generally drop the curtain as if the drama be over then the doubt and struggle of life end as if once land in the marriage country all be green and pleasant there and wife and husband have nothing to do but to link each other s arm together and wander gently downwards towards old age in happy and perfect fruition
but our little amelia be just on the bank of her new country and be already look anxiously back towards the sad friendly figure wave farewell to she across the stream from the other distant shore
in honour of the young bride s arrival her mother think it necessary to prepare i donot know what festive entertainment and after the first ebullition of talk take leave of mrs
george osborne for a while and dive down to the low region of the house to a sort of kitchen-parlour occupy by mr
and mrs
clapp and in the evening when her dish be wash and her curl-paper remove by miss flannigan the irish servant there to take measure for the preparing of a magnificent ornament tea
all people have their way of express kindness and it seem to mrs
sedley that a muffin and a quantity of orange marmalade spread out in a little cut-glass saucer would be peculiarly agreeable refreshment to amelia in her most interesting situation
while these delicacy be be transact below amelia leave the drawing-room walk upstairs and find herself she scarce know how in the little room which she have occupy before her marriage and in that very chair in which she have pass so many bitter hour
she sink back in its arm as if it be an old friend and fall to think over the past week and the life beyond it
already to be look sadly and vaguely back always to be pin for something which when obtain bring doubt and sadness rather than pleasure here be the lot of our poor little creature and harmless lose wanderer in the great struggle crowd of vanity fair
here she sate and recall to herself fondly that image of george to which she have kneel before marriage
do she own to herself how different the real man be from that superb young hero whom she have worship
it require many many year--and a man must be very bad indeed--before a woman s pride and vanity will let she own to such a confession
then rebecca s twinkle green eye and baleful smile light upon she and fill she with dismay
and so she sate for awhile indulge in her usual mood of selfish brooding in that very listless melancholy attitude in which the honest maid-servant have find she on the day when she bring up the letter in which george renew his offer of marriage
she look at the little white bed which have be her a few day before and think she would like to sleep in it that night and wake as formerly with her mother smile over she in the morning then she think with terror of the great funereal damask pavilion in the vast and dingy state bedroom which be await she at the grand hotel in cavendish square
dear little white bed
how many a long night have she weep on its pillow
how she have despair and hop to die there and now be not all her wish accomplish and the lover of whom she have despair her own for ever
kind mother
how patiently and tenderly she have watch round that bed
she go and kneel down by the bedside and there this wounded and timorous but gentle and loving soul seek for consolation where as yet it must be own our little girl have but seldom look for it
love have be her faith hitherto and the sad bleed disappoint heart begin to feel the want of another consoler
have we a right to repeat or to overhear her prayer
these brother be secret and out of the domain of vanity fair in which our story lie
but this may be say that when the tea be finally announce our young lady come downstairs a great deal more cheerful that she do not despond or deplore her fate or think about george s coldness or rebecca s eye as she have be willnot to do of late
she go downstairs and kiss her father and mother and talk to the old gentleman and make he more merry than he have be for many a day
she sate down at the piano which dobbin have buy for she and sing over all her father s favourite old song

kean in shylock

he have a perfect belief in his own mind that his father would relent before very long
how could any parent be obdurate for a length of time against such a paragon as he be

and if not

chapter xxviii in which amelia invades the low countries the regiment with its officer be to be transport in ship provide by his majesty s government for the occasion and in two day after the festive assembly at mrs
o dowd s apartment in the midst of cheer from all the east india ship in the river and the military on shore the band play god save the king the officer wave their hat and the crew hurrah gallantly the transport go down the river and proceed under convoy to ostend
meanwhile the gallant jos have agree to escort his sister and the major s wife the bulk of whose good and chattel include the famous bird of paradise and turban be with the regimental baggage so that our two heroine drive pretty much unencumbered to ramsgate where there be plenty of packet ply in one of which they have a speedy passage to ostend
that period of jos s life which now ensue be so full of incident that it serve he for conversation for many year after and even the tiger-hunt story be put aside for more stirring narrative which he have to tell about the great campaign of waterloo
as soon as he have agree to escort his sister abroad it be remark that he cease shave his upper lip
at chatham he follow the parade and drill with great assiduity
he listen with the utmost attention to the conversation of his brother officer as he call they in after day sometimes and learn as many military name as he could
in these study the excellent mrs
o dowd be of great assistance to he and on the day finally when they embark on board the lovely rose which be to carry they to their destination he make his appearance in a braid frock-coat and duck trouser with a forage cap ornament with a smart gold band
have his carriage with he and inform everybody on board confidentially that he be go to join the duke of wellington s army folk mistook he for a great personage a commissary-general or a government courier at the very least
he suffer hugely on the voyage during which the lady be likewise prostrate but amelia be bring to life again as the packet make ostend by the sight of the transport convey her regiment which enter the harbour almost at the same time with the lovely rose
jos go in a collapse state to an inn while captain dobbin escort the lady and then busy himself in free jos s carriage and luggage from the ship and the custom-house for mr
jos be at present without a servant osborne s man and his own pamper menial have conspire together at chatham and refuse point-blank to cross the water
this revolt which come very suddenly and on the last day so alarmed mr
sedley junior that he be on the point of give up the expedition but captain dobbin who make himself immensely officious in the business jos say rat he and laugh at he soundly the mustachio be grow in advance and jos finally be persuade to embark
in place of the well-breed and well-feed london domestic who could only speak english dobbin procure for jos s party a swarthy little belgian servant who could speak no language at all but who by his bustling behaviour and by invariably address mr
sedley as my lord speedily acquire that gentleman s favour
time be alter at ostend now of the britons who go thither very few look like lord or act like those member of our hereditary aristocracy
they seem for the most part shabby in attire dingy of linen lover of billiard and brandy and cigar and greasy ordinary
but it may be say as a rule that every englishman in the duke of wellington s army pay his way
the remembrance of such a fact surely become a nation of shopkeeper
it be a blessing for a commerce-love country to be overrun by such an army of customer and to have such creditable warrior to feed
and the country which they come to protect be not military
for a long period of history they have let other people fight there
when the present writer go to survey with eagle glance the field of waterloo we ask the conductor of the diligence a portly warlike-look veteran whether he have be at the battle
pas si bete such an answer and sentiment as no frenchman would own to--be his reply
but on the other hand the postilion who drive we be a viscount a son of some bankrupt imperial general who accept a pennyworth of beer on the road
the moral be surely a good one
this flat flourishing easy country never could have look more rich and prosperous than in that opening summer of 1815 when its green field and quiet city be enliven by multiply red-coat when its wide chaussee swarm with brilliant english equipage when its great canal-boat glide by rich pasture and pleasant quaint old village by old chateaux lie amongst old tree be all crowd with well-to-do english traveller when the soldier who drink at the village inn not only drank but pay his score and donald the highlander billet in the flemish farm-house rock the baby s cradle while jean and jeannette be out get in the hay
as our painter be bent on military subject just now i throw out this as a good subject for the pencil to illustrate the principle of an honest english war
all look as brilliant and harmless as a hyde park review
meanwhile napoleon screen behind his curtain of frontier-fortress be prepare for the outbreak which be to drive all these orderly people into fury and blood and lie so many of they low
everybody have such a perfect feeling of confidence in the leader for the resolute faith which the duke of wellington have inspire in the whole english nation be as intense as that more frantic enthusiasm with which at one time the french regard napoleon the country seem in so perfect a state of orderly defence and the help at hand in case of need so near and overwhelming that alarm be unknown and our traveller among whom two be naturally of a very timid sort be like all the other multiply english tourist entirely at ease
the famous regiment with so many of whose officer we have make acquaintance be draft in canal boat to bruges and ghent thence to march to brussels
jos accompany the lady in the public boat the which all old traveller in flanders must remember for the luxury and accommodation they afford
so prodigiously good be the eating and drink on board these sluggish but most comfortable vessel that there be legend extant of an english traveller who come to belgium for a week and travel in one of these boat be so delighted with the fare there that he go backwards and forwards from ghent to bruge perpetually until the railroad be invent when he drown himself on the last trip of the passage-boat
jos s death be not to be of this sort but his comfort be exceed and mrs
o dowd insist that he only want her sister glorvina to make his happiness complete
he sate on the roof of the cabin all day drink flemish beer shout for isidor his servant and talk gallantly to the lady
his courage be prodigious
boney attack we he cry
my dear creature my poor emmy donot be frightened
therebe no danger
the ally will be in paris in two month i tell you when iwill take you to dine in the palais royal by jove
there be three hundred thousand rooshians i tell you now enter france by mayence and the rhine--three hundred thousand under wittgenstein and barclay de tolly my poor love

those who like to lay down the history-book and to speculate upon what might have happen in the world but for the fatal occurrence of what actually do take place a most puzzling amusing ingenious and profitable kind of meditation have no doubt often think to themselves what a specially bad time napoleon take to come back from elba and to let loose his eagle from gulf san juan to notre dame
the historian on our side tell we that the army of the allied power be all providentially on a war-foot and ready to bear down at a moment s notice upon the elban emperor
the august jobber assemble at vienna and carve out the kingdom of europe accord to their wisdom have such cause of quarrel among themselves as might have set the army which have overcome napoleon to fight against each other but for the return of the object of unanimous hatred and fear
this monarch have an army in full force because he have job to himself poland and be determined to keep it another have rob half saxony and be bent upon maintain his acquisition italy be the object of a third s solicitude
each be protest against the rapacity of the other and could the corsican but have wait in prison until all these party be by the ear he might have return and reign unmoleste

when our traveller arrive at brussels in which their regiment be quarter a great piece of good fortune as all say they find themselves in one of the gay and most brilliant little capital in europe and where all the vanity fair booth be lay out with the most tempting liveliness and splendour

those who know the present lieutenant-general sir george tufto k c b and have see he as they may on most day in the season pad and in stay strut down pall mall with a rickety swagger on his high-heel lacquered boot leer under the bonnet of passer-by or rid a showy chestnut and ogle brougham in the parks--those who know the present sir george tufto would hardly recognise the daring peninsular and waterloo officer
he have thick curl brown hair and black eyebrow now and his whisker be of the deep purple
he be light-haired and bald in 1815 and stouter in the person and in the limb which especially have shrink very much of late

of this defeat he and a vast number of other person in brussels and belgium do not make the slight doubt

in the early spring of 1816 galignani s journal contain the follow announcement in an interesting corner of the paper on the 26 th of march--the lady of lieutenant-colonel crawley of the life guards green--of a son and heir this event be copy into the london paper out of which miss briggs read the statement to miss crawley at breakfast at brighton
the intelligence expect as it might have be cause a crisis in the affair of the crawley family
the spinster s rage rise to its height and send instantly for pitt her nephew and for the lady southdown from brunswick square she request an immediate celebration of the marriage which have be so long pende between the two family
and she announce that it be her intention to allow the young couple a thousand a year during her lifetime at the expiration of which the bulk of her property would be settle upon her nephew and her dear niece lady jane crawley
waxy come down to ratify the deed--lord southdown give away his sister--she be marry by a bishop and not by the rev

pitt and his wife come therefore and live with miss crawley and greatly to the annoyance of poor pitt who conceive himself a most injure character--be subject to the humour of his aunt on one side and of his mother-in-law on the other lady southdown from her neighbouring house reign over the whole family--pitt lady jane miss crawley briggs bowls firkin and all
she pitilessly dose they with her tract and her medicine she dismiss creamer she instal rodgers and soon strip miss crawley of even the semblance of authority

peace to thee kind and selfish vain and generous old heathen we shall see thee no more

chapter xxxv widow and mother the news of the great fight of quatre bras and waterloo reach england at the same time
the gazette first publish the result of the two battle at which glorious intelligence all england thrill with triumph and fear
particular then follow and after the announcement of the victory come the list of the wounded and the slain
who can tell the dread with which that catalogue be open and read
fancy at every village and homestead almost through the three kingdom the great news come of the battle in flanders and the feeling of exultation and gratitude bereavement and sicken dismay when the list of the regimental loss be go through and it become know whether the dear friend and relative have escape or fall
anybody who will take the trouble of look back to a file of the newspaper of the time must even now feel at second-hand this breathless pause of expectation
the list of casualty be carry on from day to day you stop in the midst as in a story which be to be continue in our next
think what the feeling must have be as those paper follow each other fresh from the press and if such an interest could be feel in our country and about a battle where but twenty thousand of our people be engage think of the condition of europe for twenty year before where people be fight not by thousand but by million each one of whom as he strike his enemy wound horribly some other innocent heart far away
the news which that famous gazette bring to the osbornes give a dreadful shock to the family and its chief
the girl indulge unrestrained in their grief
the gloom-stricken old father be still more bear down by his fate and sorrow
he strove to think that a judgment be on the boy for his disobedience
he dare not own that the severity of the sentence frighten he and that its fulfilment have come too soon upon his curse
sometimes a shudder terror strike he as if he have be the author of the doom which he have call down on his son
there be a chance before of reconciliation
the boy s wife might have die or he might have come back and say father i have sin
but there be no hope now
he stand on the other side of the gulf impassable haunt his parent with sad eye
he remember they once before so in a fever when every one think the lad be die and he lie on his bed speechless and gaze with a dreadful gloom
good god
how the father clung to the doctor then and with what a sicken anxiety he follow he what a weight of grief be off his mind when after the crisis of the fever the lad recover and look at his father once more with eye that recognise he
but now there be no help or cure or chance of reconcilement above all there be no humble word to soothe vanity outrage and furious or bring to its natural flow the poison angry blood
and it be hard to say which pang it be that tear the proud father s heart most keenly--that his son should have go out of the reach of his forgiveness or that the apology which his own pride expect should have escape he
whatever his sensation might have be however the stem old man would have no confidant
he never mention his son s name to his daughter but order the elder to place all the female of the establishment in mourning and desire that the male servant should be similarly attire in deep black
all party and entertainment of course be to be put off
no communication be make to his future son-in-law whose marriage-day have be fix but there be enough in mr
osborne s appearance to prevent mr
bullock from make any inquiry or in any way press forward that ceremony
he and the lady whisper about it under their voice in the drawing-room sometimes whither the father never come
he remain constantly in his own study the whole front part of the house be close until some time after the completion of the general mourning
about three week after the 18 th of june mr
osborne s acquaintance sir william dobbin call at mr
osborne s house in russell square with a very pale and agitated face and insist upon see that gentleman
usher into his room and after a few word which neither the speaker nor the host understand the former produce from an inclosure a letter seal with a large red seal
my son major dobbin the alderman say with some hesitation despatch i a letter by an officer of the th who arrive in town to-day
my son s letter contain one for you osborne the alderman place the letter on the table and osborne star at he for a moment or two in silence
his look frighten the ambassador who after look guiltily for a little time at the grief-stricken man hurry away without another word
the letter be in george s well-know bold handwriting
it be that one which he have write before daybreak on the 16 th of june and just before he take leave of amelia
the great red seal be emblazon with the sham coat of arm which osborne have assume from the peerage with pax in bello for a motto that of the ducal house with which the vain old man try to fancy himself connect
the hand that sign it would never hold pen or sword more
the very seal that seal it have be rob from george s dead body as it lie on the field of battle
the father know nothing of this but sit and look at the letter in terrified vacancy
he almost fall when he go to open it
have you ever have a difference with a dear friend
how his letter write in the period of love and confidence sicken and rebuke you
what a dreary mourning it be to dwell upon those vehement protest of dead affection
what lie epitaph they make over the corpse of love
what dark cruel comment upon life and vanity
most of we have get or write drawer full of they
they be closet-skeleton which we keep and shun
osborne tremble long before the letter from his dead son
the poor boy s letter do not say much
he have be too proud to acknowledge the tenderness which his heart feel
he only say that on the eve of a great battle he wish to bid his father farewell and solemnly to implore his good office for the wife--it might be for the child--whom he leave behind he
he own with contrition that his irregularity and his extravagance have already waste a large part of his mother s little fortune
he thank his father for his former generous conduct and he promise he that if he fall on the field or survive it he would act in a manner worthy of the name of george osborne
his english habit pride awkwardness perhaps have prevent he from say more
his father could not see the kiss george have place on the superscription of his letter
mr
osborne drop it with the bitter deadly pang of balked affection and revenge
his son be still beloved and unforgiven
about two month afterwards however as the young lady of the family go to church with their father they remark how he take a different seat from that which he usually occupy when he choose to attend divine worship and that from his cushion opposite he look up at the wall over their head
this cause the young woman likewise to gaze in the direction towards which their father s gloomy eye point and they see an elaborate monument upon the wall where britannia be represent weep over an urn and a broken sword and a couchant lion indicate that the piece of sculpture have be erect in honour of a deceased warrior
the sculptor of those day have stock of such funereal emblem in hand as you may see still on the wall of st
paul s which be cover with hundred of these braggart heathen allegory
there be a constant demand for they during the first fifteen year of the present century
under the memorial in question be emblazon the well-know and pompous osborne arm and the inscription say that the monument be sacred to the memory of george osborne junior esq late a captain in his majesty s th regiment of foot who fall on the 18 th of june 1815 age 28 year while fight for his king and country in the glorious victory of waterloo
dulce et decorum est pro patria mori the sight of that stone agitate the nerve of the sister so much that miss maria be compel to leave the church

george the girl say to themselves as soon as their ebullition of grief be over
much conversation pass too among the acquaintance of the osborne family who know of the rupture between the son and father cause by the former s marriage as to the chance of a reconciliation with the young widow
there be bet among the gentleman both about russell square and in the city

he do not say whither but they know at once that his step would be turn towards belgium and be aware that george s widow be still in brussels
they have pretty accurate news indeed of poor amelia from lady dobbin and her daughter
our honest captain have be promote in consequence of the death of the second major of the regiment on the field and the brave o dowd who have distinguish himself greatly here as upon all occasion where he have a chance to show his coolness and valour be a colonel and companion of the bath
very many of the brave th who have suffer severely upon both day of action be still at brussels in the autumn recover of their wound
the city be a vast military hospital for month after the great battle and as man and officer begin to rally from their hurt the garden and place of public resort swarm with maim warrior old and young who just rescue out of death fall to gambling and gaiety and love-making as people of vanity fair will do
mr
osborne find out some of the th easily
he know their uniform quite well and have be use to follow all the promotion and exchange in the regiment and love to talk about it and its officer as if he have be one of the number
on the day after his arrival at brussels and as he issue from his hotel which face the park he see a soldier in the well-know facing repose on a stone bench in the garden and go and sate down tremble by the wound convalescent man
be you in captain osborne s company he say and add after a pause he be my son sir the man be not of the captain s company but he lift up his unwounded arm and touch-his cap sadly and respectfully to the haggard break-spirit gentleman who question he
the whole army donot contain a finer or a well officer the soldier say
the sergeant of the captain s company captain raymond have it now be in town though and be just well of a shot in the shoulder

but your honour know all about that--and ask your pardon the man add

osborne s presence
and the first soldier go away and after tell a comrade or two how captain osborne s father be arrive and what a free-handed generous gentleman he be they go and make good cheer with drink and feasting as long as the guineas last which have come from the proud purse of the mourn old father
in the sergeant s company who be also just convalescent osborne make the journey of waterloo and quatre bras a journey which thousand of his countryman be then take
he take the sergeant with he in his carriage and go through both field under his guidance
he see the point of the road where the regiment march into action on the 16 th and the slope down which they drive the french cavalry who be press on the retreat belgians
there be the spot where the noble captain cut down the french officer who be grapple with the young ensign for the colour the colour-sergeant have be shoot down
along this road they retreat on the next day and here be the bank at which the regiment bivouac under the rain of the night of the seventeenth
far on be the position which they take and hold during the day form time after time to receive the charge of the enemy s horseman and lie down under the shelter of the bank from the furious french cannonade
and it be at this declivity when at evening the whole english line receive the order to advance as the enemy fall back after his last charge that the captain hurraye and rush down the hill wave his sword receive a shot and fall dead
it be major dobbin who take back the captain s body to brussels the sergeant say in a low voice and have he bury as your honour know the peasant and relic-hunter about the place be scream round the pair as the soldier tell his story offer for sale all sort of memento of the fight cross and epaulet and shatter cuirass and eagle
osborne give a sumptuous reward to the sergeant when he part with he after have visit the scene of his son s last exploit
his burial-place he have already see
indeed he have drive thither immediately after his arrival at brussels
george s body lie in the pretty burial-ground of laeken near the city in which place have once visit it on a party of pleasure he have lightly express a wish to have his grave make
and there the young officer be lay by his friend in the unconsecrated corner of the garden separate by a little hedge from the temple and tower and plantation of flower and shrub under which the roman catholic dead repose
it seem a humiliation to old osborne to think that his son an english gentleman a captain in the famous british army should not be find worthy to lie in ground where mere foreigner be bury
which of we be there can tell how much vanity lurk in our warm regard for other and how selfish our love be
old osborne do not speculate much upon the mingle nature of his feeling and how his instinct and selfishness be combat together
he firmly believe that everything he do be right that he ought on all occasion to have his own way--and like the sting of a wasp or serpent his hatred rush out armed and poisonous against anything like opposition
he be proud of his hatred as of everything else
always to be right always to trample forward and never to doubt be not these the great quality with which dullness take the lead in the world
as after the drive to waterloo mr
osborne s carriage be near the gate of the city at sunset they meet another open barouche in which be a couple of lady and a gentleman and by the side of which an officer be rid

it be amelia with the lame young ensign by her side and opposite to she her faithful friend mrs
o dowd

he hat she

damn you
i do hate she

chapter xxxvi how to live well on nothing a year i suppose there be no man in this vanity fair of ours so little observant as not to think sometimes about the worldly affair of his acquaintance or so extremely charitable as not to wonder how his neighbour jones or his neighbour smith can make both end meet at the end of the year
with the utmost regard for the family for instance for i dine with they twice or thrice in the season i cannot but own that the appearance of the jenkinses in the park in the large barouche with the grenadi-footman will surprise and mystify i to my dying day for though i know the equipage be only job and all the jenkins people be on board wage yet those three man and the carriage must represent an expense of six hundred a year at the very least--and then there be the splendid dinner the two boy at eton the prize governess and master for the girl the trip abroad or to eastbourne or worthing in the autumn the annual ball with a supper from gunter s who by the way supply most of the first-rate dinner which j
give as i know very well have be invite to one of they to fill a vacant place when i see at once that these repast be very superior to the common run of entertainment for which the humbler sort of j s acquaintance get cards who i say with the most good-natured feeling in the world can help wonder how the jenkinses make out matter
what be jenkin
we all know--commissioner of the tape and sealing wax office with 1200 pound a year for a salary
have his wife a private fortune
pooh miss flint--one of eleven child of a small squire in buckinghamshire
all she ever get from her family be a turkey at christmas in exchange for which she have to board two or three of her sister in the off season and lodge and feed her brother when they come to town
how do jenkins balance his income
i say as every friend of his must say how be it that he have not be outlaw long since and that he ever come back as he do to the surprise of everybody last year from boulogne
i be here introduce to personify the world in general--the mrs
grundy of each respected reader s private circle--every one of whom can point to some family of his acquaintance who live nobody know how
many a glass of wine have we all of we drunk i have very little doubt hob-and-nobbing with the hospitable giver and wonder how the deuce he pay for it
some three or four year after his stay in paris when rawdon crawley and his wife be establish in a very small comfortable house in curzon street may fair there be scarcely one of the numerous friend whom they entertain at dinner that do not ask the above question regard they
the novelist it have be say before know everything and as i be in a situation to be able to tell the public how crawley and his wife live without any income may i entreat the public newspaper which be in the habit of extract portion of the various periodical work now publish not to reprint the follow exact narrative and calculation--of which i ought as the discoverer and at some expense too to have the benefit
my son i would say be i bless with a child--you may by deep inquiry and constant intercourse with he learn how a man live comfortably on nothing a year
but it be good not to be intimate with gentleman of this profession and to take the calculation at second hand as you do logarithm for to work they yourself depend upon it will cost you something considerable
on nothing per annum then and during a course of some two or three year of which we can afford to give but a very brief history crawley and his wife live very happily and comfortably at paris

crawley who may have be dispose during this period of elation to slight the people--honest young military man mostly--who form her husband s chief society
but the colonel yawn sadly among the duchesses and great lady of the court
the old woman who play ecarte make such a noise about a five-franc piece that it be not worth colonel crawley s while to sit down at a card-table
the wit of their conversation he could not appreciate be ignorant of their language
and what good could his wife get he urge by make curtsy every night to a whole circle of princesses
he leave rebecca presently to frequent these party alone resume his own simple pursuit and amusement amongst the amiable friend of his own choice
the truth be when we say of a gentleman that he live elegantly on nothing a year we use the word nothing to signify something unknown meaning simply that we donot know how the gentleman in question defray the expense of his establishment
now our friend the colonel have a great aptitude for all game of chance and exercise himself as he continually do with the card the dice-box or the cue it be natural to suppose that he attain a much great skill in the use of these article than man can possess who only occasionally handle they
to use a cue at billiard well be like use a pencil or a german flute or a small-sword--you cannot master any one of these implement at first and it be only by repeat study and perseverance join to a natural taste that a man can excel in the handling of either
now crawley from be only a brilliant amateur have grow to be a consummate master of billiard
like a great general his genius use to rise with the danger and when the luck have be unfavourable to he for a whole game and the bet be consequently against he he would with consummate skill and boldness make some prodigious hit which would restore the battle and come in a victor at the end to the astonishment of everybody--of everybody that is who be a stranger to his play
those who be accustomed to see it be cautious how they stake their money against a man of such sudden resource and brilliant and overpower skill
at game of card he be equally skilful for though he would constantly lose money at the commencement of an evening play so carelessly and make such blunder that newcomer be often incline to think meanly of his talent yet when rouse to action and awaken to caution by repeat small loss it be remark that crawley s play become quite different and that he be pretty sure of beat his enemy thoroughly before the night be over
indeed very few man could say that they ever have the well of he
his success be so repeat that no wonder the envious and the vanquished speak sometimes with bitterness regard they
and as the french say of the duke of wellington who never suffer a defeat that only an astonishing series of lucky accident enable he to be an invariable winner yet even they allow that he cheat at waterloo and be enable to win the last great trick so it be hint at headquarters in england that some foul play must have take place in order to account for the continuous success of colonel crawley

chapter xxxvii the subject continue in the first place and as a matter of the great necessity we be bind to describe how a house may be get for nothing a year

of course they do not intend to occupy permanently an apartment so splendid

the footman occasionally lounge at the area rail treat he with respect the cook take her green stuff at his house and call he mr

swishtail s sugar-cane lodge and little matilda to miss peckover s laurentinum house clapham

this be the way then crawley get his house for nothing for though raggles have to pay taxis and rate and the interest of the mortgage to the brother butler and the insurance of his life and the charge for his child at school and the value of the meat and drink which his own family--and for a time that of colonel crawley too--consume and though the poor wretch be utterly ruin by the transaction his child be fling on the street and himself drive into the fleet prison yet somebody must pay even for gentleman who live for nothing a year--and so it be this unlucky raggles be make the representative of colonel crawley s defective capital
i wonder how many family be drive to roguery and to ruin by great practitioner in crawlers way how many great nobleman rob their petty tradesman condescend to swindle their poor retainer out of wretched little sum and cheat for a few shilling
when we read that a noble nobleman have leave for the continent or that another noble nobleman have an execution in his house--and that one or other owe six or seven million the defeat seem glorious even and we respect the victim in the vastness of his ruin
but who pity a poor barber who cannot get his money for powder the footman s head or a poor carpenter who have ruin himself by fix up ornament and pavilion for my lady s dejeuner or the poor devil of a tailor whom the steward patronize and who have pledge all he be worth and more to get the livery ready which my lord have do he the honour to bespeak
when the great house tumble down these miserable wretch fall under it unnoticed as they say in the old legend before a man go to the devil himself he send plenty of other soul thither
rawdon and his wife generously give their patronage to all such of miss crawley s tradesman and purveyor as choose to serve they
some be willing enough especially the poor one
it be wonderful to see the pertinacity with which the washerwoman from tooting bring the cart every saturday and her bill week after week
mr
raggles himself have to supply the greengrocery
the bill for servant porter at the fortune of war public house be a curiosity in the chronicle of beer
every servant also be owe the great part of his wage and thus keep up perforce an interest in the house
nobody in fact be pay
not the blacksmith who open the lock nor the glazier who mend the pane nor the jobber who let the carriage nor the groom who drive it nor the butcher who provide the leg of mutton nor the coal which roast it nor the cook who baste it nor the servant who eat it and this i be give to understand be not unfrequently the way in which people live elegantly on nothing a year
in a little town such thing cannot be do without remark
we know there the quantity of milk our neighbour take and espy the joint or the fowl which be go in for his dinner
so probably 200 and 202 in curzon street might know what be go on in the house between they the servant communicate through the area-railing but crawley and his wife and his friend do not know 200 and 202
when you come to 201 there be a hearty welcome a kind smile a good dinner and a jolly shake of the hand from the host and hostess there just for all the world as if they have be undisputed master of three or four thousand a year--and so they be not in money but in produce and labour--if they do not pay for the mutton they have it if they do not give bullion in exchange for their wine how should we know
never be well claret at any man s table than at honest rawdon s dinner more gay and neatly serve

you see demure chariot at her door out of which step very great people
you behold her carriage in the park surround by dandy of note
the little box in the third tier of the opera be crowd with head constantly change but it must be confess that the lady hold aloof from she and that their door be shut to our little adventurer
with regard to the world of female fashion and its custom the present writer of course can only speak at second hand
a man can no more penetrate or under-stand those mystery than he can know what the lady talk about when they go upstairs after dinner
it be only by inquiry and perseverance that one sometimes get hint of those secret and by a similar diligence every person who tread the pall mall pavement and frequent the club of this metropolis know either through his own experience or through some acquaintance with whom he play at billiard or share the joint something about the genteel world of london and how as there be man such as rawdon crawley whose position we mention before who cut a good figure to the eye of the ignorant world and to the apprentice in the park who behold they consort with the most notorious dandy there so there be lady who may be call man s woman be welcome entirely by all the gentleman and cut or slight by all their wife

but while simple folk who be out of the world or country people with a taste for the genteel behold these lady in their seem glory in public place or envy they from afar off person who be well instruct could inform they that these envy lady have no more chance of establish themselves in society than the benighted squire s wife in somersetshire who read of their doing in the morning post
man live about london be aware of these awful truth
you hear how pitilessly many lady of seem rank and wealth be exclude from this society the frantic effort which they make to enter this circle the meanness to which they submit the insult which they undergo be matter of wonder to those who take human or womankind for a study and the pursuit of fashion under difficulty would be a fine theme for any very great person who have the wit the leisure and the knowledge of the english language necessary for the compiling of such a history

he have a thousand anecdote about the famous battle he know the position of every regiment and the loss which each have incur
he do not deny that he have be concern in those victory--that he have be with the army and carry despatch for the duke of wellington
and he describe what the duke do and say on every conceivable moment of the day of waterloo with such an accurate knowledge of his grace s sentiment and proceeding that it be clear he must have be by the conqueror s side throughout the day though as a non-combatant his name be not mention in the public document relative to the battle

sedley s speculation in life subsequent to his bankruptcy do not by any mean retrieve the break old gentleman s fortune
he try to be a wine-merchant a coal-merchant a commission lottery agent c c
he send round prospectus to his friend whenever he take a new trade and order a new brass plate for the door and talk pompously about make his fortune still
but fortune never come back to the feeble and stricken old man
one by one his friend drop off and be weary of buy dear coal and bad wine from he and there be only his wife in all the world who fancy when he totter off to the city of a morning that he be still do any business there
at evening he crawl slowly back and he use to go of night to a little club at a tavern where he dispose of the finance of the nation
it be wonderful to hear he talk about million and agio and discount and what rothschild be do and baring brothers

clapp our old acquaintance respect the old gentleman

but the sedleys be always a proud family you and i my dear reader may drop into this condition one day for have not many of our friend attain it

he have be know to treat some of these non-commission gentleman to a glass of porter and indeed in their first sunday walk be disposed to spoil little georgy sadly gorge the boy with apple and parliament to the detriment of his health--until amelia declare that george should never go out with his grandpapa unless the latter promise solemnly and on his honour not to give the child any cake lollipop or stall produce whatever
between mrs
sedley and her daughter there be a sort of coolness about this boy and a secret jealousy--for one evening in george s very early day amelia who have be seat at work in their little parlour scarcely remark that the old lady have quit the room run upstairs instinctively to the nursery at the cry of the child who have be asleep until that moment--and there find mrs
sedley in the act of surreptitiously administer daffy s elixir to the infant
amelia the gentle and sweetest of everyday mortal when she find this meddling with her maternal authority thrill and tremble all over with anger
her cheek ordinarily pale now flush up until they be as red as they use to be when she be a child of twelve year old
she seize the baby out of her mother s arm and then grasp at the bottle leave the old lady gap at she furious and hold the guilty tea-spoon
amelia fling the bottle crash into the fire-place
i will not have baby poison mamma cry emmy rock the infant about violently with both her arm round he and turn with flash eye at her mother

this be the language you use to your mother
i have meet with misfortune i have sink low in life i have keep my carriage and now walk on foot but i do not know i be a murderess before and thank you for the news mamma say the poor girl who be always ready for tears-- you shouldnot be hard upon i
i--i donot mean--i mean i do not wish to say you would to any wrong to this dear child only-- oh no my love only that i be a murderess in which case i have well go to the old bailey
though i donot poison you when you be a child but give you the good of education and the most expensive master money could procure
yes ihave nurse five child and bury three and the one i love the good of all and tend through croup and teethe and measle and hooping-cough and bring up with foreign master regardless of expense and with accomplishment at minerva house--which i never have when i be a girl--when i be too glad to honour my father and mother that i might live long in the land and to be useful and not to mope all day in my room and act the fine lady--say ibe a murderess
ah mrs
osborne

in this room be all amelia s heart and treasure
here it be that she tend her boy and watch he through the many ill of childhood with a constant passion of love
the elder george return in he somehow only improve and as if come back from heaven
in a hundred little tone look and movement the child be so like his father that the widow s heart thrill as she hold he to it and he would often ask the cause of her tear

the very joy of this woman be a sort of grief or so tender at least that its expression be tear
her sensibility be so weak and tremulous that perhaps they ought not to be talk about in a book
i be tell by dr
pestler now a most flourishing lady s physician with a sumptuous dark green carriage a prospect of speedy knighthood and a house in manchester square that her grief at wean the child be a sight that would have unmanned a herod

perhaps the doctor s lady have good reason for her jealousy most woman share it of those who form the small circle of amelia s acquaintance and be quite angry at the enthusiasm with which the other sex regard she
for almost all man who come near her love she though no doubt they would be at a loss to tell you why
she be not brilliant nor witty nor wise over much nor extraordinarily handsome
but wherever she go she touch and charm every one of the male sex as invariably as she awaken the scorn and incredulity of her own sisterhood
i think it be her weakness which be her principal charm--a kind of sweet submission and softness which seem to appeal to each man she meet for his sympathy and protection

i know no sort of lying which be more frequent in vanity fair than this and it may be remark how people who practise it take credit to themselves for their hypocrisy and fancy that they be exceedingly virtuous and praiseworthy because they be able to deceive the world with regard to the extent of their mean

the scotch gardener who still linger on the premise take a pride in his wall and hot-house and indeed make a pretty good livelihood by the garden which he farm and of which he sell the produce at southampton find the ribbons eat peach on a sunshiny morning at the south-wall and have his ear box when he remonstrate about this attack on his property
he and his scotch wife and his scotch child the only respectable inhabitant of queen s crawley be force to migrate with their good and their chattel and leave the stately comfortable garden to go to waste and the flower-bed to run to seed

it benot difficult to be a country gentleman s wife rebecca think
i think i could be a good woman if i have five thousand a year
i could dawdle about in the nursery and count the apricot on the wall
i could water plant in a green-house and pick off dead leaf from the geranium
i could ask old woman about their rheumatism and order half-a-crown s worth of soup for the poor
i shouldnot miss it much out of five thousand a year
i could even drive out ten mile to dine at a neighbour s and dress in the fashion of the year before last
i could go to church and keep awake in the great family pew or go to sleep behind the curtain with my veil down if i only have practice
i could pay everybody if i have but the money
this be what the conjuror here pride themselves upon do

jane osborne keep her bedroom for many day

her father swear to she that she should not have a shilling of his money if she make any match without his concurrence and as he want a woman to keep his house he do not choose that she should marry so that she be oblige to give up all project with which cupid have any share
during her papa s life then she resign herself to the manner of existence here describe and be content to be an old maid
her sister meanwhile be have child with fine name every year and the intercourse between the two grow fainter continually
jane and i do not move in the same sphere of life mrs
bullock say
i regard she as a sister of course which mean--what do it mean when a lady say that she regard jane as a sister
it have be describe how the misses dobbin live with their father at a fine villa at denmark hill where there be beautiful graperie and peach-tree which delight little georgy osborne
the misses dobbin who drive often to brompton to see our dear amelia come sometimes to russell square too to pay a visit to their old acquaintance miss osborne
i believe it be in consequence of the command of their brother the major in india for whom their papa have a prodigious respect that they pay attention to mrs
george for the major the godfather and guardian of amelia s little boy still hop that the child s grandfather might be induce to relent towards he and acknowledge he for the sake of his son
the misses dobbin keep miss osborne acquaint with the state of amelia s affair how she be live with her father and mother how poor they be how they wonder what man and such man as their brother and dear captain osborne could find in such an insignificant little chit how she be still as heretofore a namby-pamby milk-and-water affected creature--but how the boy be really the noble little boy ever see--for the heart of all woman warm towards young child and the sour spinster be kind to they
one day after great entreaty on the part of the misses dobbin amelia allow little george to go and pass a day with they at denmark hill--a part of which day she spend herself in writing to the major in india
she congratulate he on the happy news which his sister have just convey to she
she pray for his prosperity and that of the bride he have choose
she thank he for a thousand thousand kind office and proof of stead fast friendship to she in her affliction
she tell he the last news about little georgy and how he be go to spend that very day with his sister in the country
she underline the letter a great deal and she sign herself affectionately his friend amelia osborne
she forget to send any message of kindness to lady o dowd as she willnot be--and do not mention glorvina by name and only in italic as the major s bride for whom she beg blessing
but the news of the marriage remove the reserve which she have keep up towards he
she be glad to be able to own and feel how warmly and gratefully she regard he--and as for the idea of be jealous of glorvina glorvina indeed amelia would have scout it if an angel from heaven have hint it to she
that night when georgy come back in the pony-carriage in which he rejoice and in which he be drive by sir wm
dobbin s old coachman he have round his neck a fine gold chain and watch
he say an old lady not pretty have give it he who cry and kiss he a great deal
but he donot like she
he like grape very much
and he only like his mamma
amelia shrink and start the timid soul feel a presentiment of terror when she hear that the relation of the child s father have see he
miss osborne come back to give her father his dinner
he have make a good speculation in the city and be rather in a good humour that day and chance to remark the agitation under which she labour
whatbe the matter miss osborne he deign to say
the woman burst into tear
oh sir she say ihave see little george
he be as beautiful as an angel--and so like he the old man opposite to she do not say a word but flush up and begin to tremble in every limb
chapter xliii in which the reader have to double the cape the astonished reader must be call upon to transport himself ten thousand mile to the military station of bundlegunge in the madras division of our indian empire where our gallant old friend of the th regiment be quarter under the command of the brave colonel sir michael o dowd
time have deal kindly with that stout officer as it do ordinarily with man who have good stomach and good temper and be not perplex over much by fatigue of the brain
the colonel play a good knife and fork at tiffin and resume those weapon with great success at dinner
he smoke his hookah after both meal and puff as quietly while his wife scold he as he do under the fire of the french at waterloo
age and heat have not diminish the activity or the eloquence of the descendant of the malonys and the molloys
her ladyship our old acquaintance be as much at home at madras as at brussels in the cantonment as under the tent
on the march you see she at the head of the regiment seat on a royal elephant a noble sight
mount on that beast she have be into action with tiger in the jungle she have be receive by native prince who have welcome she and glorvina into the recess of their zenana and offer she shawl and jewel which it go to her heart to refuse
the sentry of all arm salute she wherever she make her appearance and she touch her hat gravely to their salutation
lady o dowd be one of the great lady in the presidency of madras--her quarrel with lady smith wife of sir minos smith the puisne judge be still remember by some at madras when the colonel s lady snap her finger in the judge s lady s face and say she d never walk behind ever a beggarly civilian
even now though it be five-and-twenty year ago people remember lady o dowd perform a jig at government house where she dance down two aides-de-camp a major of madras cavalry and two gentleman of the civil service and persuade by major dobbin c b second in command of the th to retire to the supper-room lassata nondum satiata recessit
peggy o dowd be indeed the same as ever kind in act and think impetuous in temper eager to command a tyrant over her michael a dragon amongst all the lady of the regiment a mother to all the young man whom she tend in their sickness defend in all their scrape and with whom lady peggy be immensely popular
but the subalterns and captains lady the major be unmarried cabal against she a good deal
they say that glorvina give herself air and that peggy herself be ill tolerably domineering
she interfere with a little congregation which mrs
kirk have get up and laugh the young man away from her sermon state that a soldier s wife have no business to be a parson--that mrs

she abruptly put a termination to a flirtation which lieutenant stubble of the regiment have commence with the surgeon s wife threaten to come down upon stubble for the money which he have borrow from she for the young fellow be still of an extravagant turn unless he break off at once and go to the cape on sick leave
on the other hand she house and shelter mrs
posky who flee from her bungalow one night pursue by her infuriate husband wield his second brandy bottle and actually carry posky through the delirium tremen and break he of the habit of drinking which have grow upon that officer as all evil habit will grow upon man
in a word in adversity she be the good of comforter in good fortune the most troublesome of friend have a perfectly good opinion of herself always and an indomitable resolution to have her own way
among other point she have make up her mind that glorvina should marry our old friend dobbin
mrs
o dowd know the major s expectation and appreciate his good quality and the high character which he enjoy in his profession
glorvina a very handsome fresh-colour black-haired blue-eyed young lady who could ride a horse or play a sonata with any girl out of the county cork seem to be the very person destine to insure dobbin s happiness--much more than that poor good little weak-spur ted amelia about whom he use to take on so look at glorvina enter a room mrs
o dowd would say and compare she with that poor mrs

she have have a season in dublin and who know how many in cork killarney and mallow
she have flirt with all the marriageable officer whom the depot of her country afford and all the bachelor squire who seem eligible
she have be engage to be marry a half-score time in ireland besides the clergyman at bath who use she so ill

o dowd who be stay there while the major of the regiment be in command at the station
everybody admire she there everybody dance with she but no one propose who be worth the marrying--one or two exceedingly young subaltern sigh after she and a beardless civilian or two but she reject these as beneath her pretension--and other and young virgin than glorvina be marry before she
there be woman and handsome woman too who have this fortune in life
they fall in love with the utmost generosity they ride and walk with half the army-list though they draw near to forty and yet the misses o grady be the misses o grady still glorvina persist that but for lady o dowd s unlucky quarrel with the judge s lady she would have make a good match at madras where old mr
chutney who be at the head of the civil service and who afterwards marry miss dolby a young lady only thirteen year of age who have just arrive from school in europe be just at the point of propose to she
well although lady o dowd and glorvina quarrel a great number of time every day and upon almost every conceivable subject--indeed if mick o dowd have not possess the temper of an angel two such woman constantly about his ear would have drive he out of his sens--yet they agree between themselves on this point that glorvina should marry major dobbin and be determined that the major should have no rest until the arrangement be bring about
undismaye by forty or fifty previous defeat glorvina lay siege to he
she sing irish melody at he unceasingly
she ask he so frequently and pathetically will ye come to the bower
that it be a wonder how any man of feeling could have resist the invitation
she be never tired of inquire if sorrow have his young day fade and be ready to listen and weep like desdemona at the story of his danger and his campaign
it have be say that our honest and dear old friend use to perform on the flute in private glorvina insist upon have duet with he and lady o dowd would rise and artlessly quit the room when the young couple be so engaged

the whole cantonment see they set out and return
she be constantly write note over to he at his house borrow his book and score with her great pencil-mark such passage of sentiment or humour as awaken her sympathy
she borrow his horse his servant his spoon and palanquin--no wonder that public rumour assign she to he and that the major s sister in england should fancy they be about to have a sister-in-law
dobbin who be thus vigorously besiege be in the meanwhile in a state of the most odious tranquillity

tozer s piano because itbe the most handy instrument in the station

as for sir michael o dowd though his lady and her sister both urge he to call upon the major to explain himself and not keep on torture a poor innocent girl in that shameful way the old soldier refuse point-blank to have anything to do with the conspiracy
faith the major s big enough to choose for himself sir michael say hewill ask ye when he want ye or else he would turn the matter off jocularly declare that dobbin be too young to keep house and have write home to ask lave of his mamma nay he go far and in private communication with his major would caution and rally he cry mind your oi dob my boy they girl be bent on mischief--i lady have just get a box of gown from europe and therebe a pink satin for glorvina which will finish ye dob if itbe in the power of woman or satin to move ye but the truth be neither beauty nor fashion could conquer he
our honest friend have but one idea of a woman in his head and that one do not in the least resemble miss glorvina o dowd in pink satin
a gentle little woman in black with large eye and brown hair seldom speak save when speak to and then in a voice not the least resemble miss glorvina s--a soft young mother tend an infant and beckon the major up with a smile to look at he--a rosy-cheeked lass come singing into the room in russell square or hang on george osborne s arm happy and loving--there be but this image that fill our honest major s mind by day and by night and reign over it always
very likely amelia be not like the portrait the major have form of she there be a figure in a book of fashion which his sister have in england and with which william have make away privately paste it into the lid of his desk and fancy he see some resemblance to mrs
osborne in the print whereas i have see it and can vouch that it be but the picture of a high-waiste gown with an impossible doll s face simper over it--and perhaps mr

but what man in love of we be well informed or be he much happy when he see and own his delusion
dobbin be under this spell

this letter which arrive by the very same ship which bring out lady o dowd s box of millinery from london and which you may be sure dobbin open before any one of the other packet which the mail bring he put the receiver into such a state of mind that glorvina and her pink satin and everything belong to she become perfectly odious to he
the major curse the talk of woman and the sex in general
everything annoy he that day--the parade be insufferably hot and wearisome
good heaven
be a man of intellect to waste his life day after day inspect cross-belt and put fool through their manoeuvre
the senseless chatter of the young man at mess be more than ever jarring

the joke about the table fill he with shame

and after the boisterous dulness of the mess-table the quarrel and scandal of the lady of the regiment
it be unbearable shameful
o amelia amelia he think you to whom i have be so faithful--you reproach i

he would like to have do with life and its vanity altogether--so bootless and unsatisfactory the struggle so cheerless and dreary the prospect seem to he
he lie all that night sleepless and yearn to go home

he read over and over all the letter which he ever have from she--letter of business relative to the little property which he have make she believe her husband have leave to she--brief note of invitation--every scrap of write that she have ever send to he--how cold how kind how hopeless how selfish they be
have there be some kind gentle soul near at hand who could read and appreciate this silent generous heart who know but that the reign of amelia might have be over and that friend william s love might have flow into a kind channel
but there be only glorvina of the jetty ringlet with whom his intercourse be familiar and this dash young woman be not bent upon love the major but rather on make the major admire her--a most vain and hopeless task too at least consider the mean that the poor girl possess to carry it out
she curl her hair and show her shoulder at he as much as to say do ye ever see such jet ringlet and such a complexion
she grin at he so that he might see that every tooth in her head be sound--and he never heed all these charm
very soon after the arrival of the box of millinery and perhaps indeed in honour of it lady o dowd and the lady of the king s regiment give a ball to the company s regiments and the civilian at the station

so these two be each exemplify the vanity of this life and each longing for what he or she could not get
