mamma send her love with that of your affectionate ann dobbin chapter xliv a round-about chapter between london and hampshire our old friend the crawleys family house in great gaunt street still bear over its front the hatchment which have be place there as a token of mourning for sir pitt crawley s demise yet this heraldic emblem be in itself a very splendid and gaudy piece of furniture and all the rest of the mansion become more brilliant than it have ever be during the late baronet s reign
the black outer-coating of the brick be remove and they appear with a cheerful blush face streak with white the old bronze lion of the knocker be gild handsomely the railing paint and the dismallest house in great gaunt street become the smart in the whole quarter before the green leaf in hampshire have replace those yellow one which be on the tree in queen s crawley avenue when old sir pitt crawley pass under they for the last time
a little woman with a carriage to correspond be perpetually see about this mansion an elderly spinster accompany by a little boy also might be remark come thither daily
it be miss briggs and little rawdon whose business it be to see to the inward renovation of sir pitt s house to superintend the female band engage in stitch the blind and hanging to poke and rummage in the drawer and cupboard cram with the dirty relic and congregate trumpery of a couple of generation of lady crawleys and to take inventory of the china the glass and other property in the closet and store-room
mrs
rawdon crawley be general-in-chief over these arrangement with full order from sir pitt to sell barter confiscate or purchase furniture and she enjoy herself not a little in an occupation which give full scope to her taste and ingenuity
the renovation of the house be determine upon when sir pitt come to town in november to see his lawyer and when he pass nearly a week in curzon street under the roof of his affectionate brother and sister
he have put up at an hotel at first but becky as soon as she hear of the baronet s arrival go off alone to greet he and return in an hour to curzon street with sir pitt in the carriage by her side
it be impossible sometimes to resist this artless little creature s hospitality so kindly be they press so frankly and amiably offer
becky seize pitt s hand in a transport of gratitude when he agree to come
thank you she say squeeze it and look into the baronet s eye who blush a good deal how happy this will make rawdon she bustle up to pitt s bedroom lead on the servant who be carry his trunk thither
she come in herself laugh with a coal-scuttle out of her own room
a fire be blaze already in sir pitt s apartment it be miss briggs s room by the way who be send upstairs to sleep with the maid
i know i should bring you she say with pleasure beam in her glance
indeed she be really sincerely happy at have he for a guest
becky make rawdon dine out once or twice on business while pitt stay with they and the baronet pass the happy evening alone with she and briggs
she go downstairs to the kitchen and actually cook little dish for he
benot it a good salmi she say i make it for you
i can make you well dish than that and will when you come to see i everything you do you do well say the baronet gallantly
the salmi be excellent indeed a poor man s wife rebecca reply gaily must make herself useful you know on which her brother-in-law vow that she be fit to be the wife of an emperor and that to be skilful in domestic duty be surely one of the most charming of woman s quality and sir pitt thought with something like mortification of lady jane at home and of a certain pie which she have insist on make and serve to he at dinner--a most abominable pie
besides the salmi which be make of lord steyne s pheasant from his lordship s cottage of stillbrook becky give her brother-in-law a bottle of white wine some that rawdon have bring with he from france and have pick up for nothing the little story-teller say whereas the liquor be in truth some white hermitage from the marquis of steyne s famous cellar which bring fire into the baronet s pallid cheek and a glow into his feeble frame
then when he have drink up the bottle of petit vin blanc she give he her hand and take he up to the drawing-room and make he snug on the sofa by the fire and let he talk as she listen with the tender kindly interest sit by he and hem a shirt for her dear little boy
whenever mrs
rawdon wish to be particularly humble and virtuous this little shirt use to come out of her work-box
it have get to be too small for rawdon long before it be finish
well rebecca listen to pitt she talk to he she sing to he she coax he and cuddle he so that he find himself more and more glad every day to get back from the lawyer s at gray s inn to the blaze fire in curzon street--a gladness in which the man of law likewise participate for pitt s harangue be of the long--and so that when he go away he feel quite a pang at depart
how pretty she look kiss her hand to he from the carriage and wave her handkerchief when he have take his place in the mail
she put the handkerchief to her eye once
he pull his sealskin cap over his as the coach drive away and sink back he think to himself how she respect he and how he deserve it and how rawdon be a foolish dull fellow who donot half-appreciate his wife and how mum and stupid his own wife be compare to that brilliant little becky
becky have hint every one of these thing herself perhaps but so delicately and gently that you hardly know when or where
and before they part it be agree that the house in london should be redecorate for the next season and that the brother family should meet again in the country at christmas
i wish you could have get a little money out of he rawdon say to his wife moodily when the baronet be go
i should like to give something to old raggles hang if i shouldnot
it benot right you know that the old fellow should be keep out of all his money
it may be inconvenient and he might let to somebody else besides we you know tell he say becky that as soon as sir pitt s affair be settle everybody will be pay and give he a little something on account
herebe a cheque that pitt leave for the boy and she take from her bag and give her husband a paper which his brother have hand over to she on behalf of the little son and heir of the young branch of the crawleys
the truth be she have try personally the ground on which her husband express a wish that she should venture--try it ever so delicately and find it unsafe
even at a hint about embarrassment sir pitt crawley be off and alarm
and he begin a long speech explain how straiten he himself be in money matter how the tenant would not pay how his father s affair and the expense attendant upon the demise of the old gentleman have involve he how he want to pay off incumbrance and how the banker and agent be overdraw and pitt crawley end by make a compromise with his sister-in-law and give she a very small sum for the benefit of her little boy
pitt know how poor his brother and his brother s family must be
it could not have escape the notice of such a cool and experienced old diplomatist that rawdon s family have nothing to live upon and that house and carriage be not to be keep for nothing
he know very well that he be the proprietor or appropriator of the money which accord to all proper calculation ought to have fall to his young brother and he have we may be sure some secret pang of remorse within he which warn he that he ought to perform some act of justice or let we say compensation towards these disappoint relation
a just decent man not without brain who say his prayer and know his catechism and do his duty outwardly through life he could not be otherwise than aware that something be due to his brother at his hand and that morally he be rawdon s debtor
but as one read in the column of the times newspaper every now and then queer announcement from the chancellor of the exchequer acknowledge the receipt of 50 pound from a
b or 10 pound from w
t as conscience-money on account of taxis due by the say a
b
or w
t which payment the penitent beg the right honourable gentleman to acknowledge through the medium of the public press--so be the chancellor no doubt and the reader likewise always perfectly sure that the above-name a
b
and w
t
be only pay a very small instalment of what they really owe and that the man who send up a twenty-pound note have very likely hundred or thousand more for which he ought to account
such at least be my feeling when i see a
b
or w
t s insufficient act of repentance
and i have no doubt that pitt crawley s contrition or kindness if you will towards his young brother by whom he have so much profit be only a very small dividend upon the capital sum in which he be indebted to rawdon
not everybody be willing to pay even so much
to part with money be a sacrifice beyond almost all man endow with a sense of order
there be scarcely any man alive who do not think himself meritorious for give his neighbour five pound
thriftless give not from a beneficent pleasure in giving but from a lazy delight in spending
he would not deny himself one enjoyment not his opera-stall not his horse not his dinner not even the pleasure of give lazarus the five pound

chapter xlv between hampshire and london sir pitt crawley have do more than repair fence and restore dilapidate lodge on the queen s crawley estate
like a wise man he have set to work to rebuild the injure popularity of his house and stop up the gap and ruin in which his name have be leave by his disreputable and thriftless old predecessor
he be elect for the borough speedily after his father s demise a magistrate a member of parliament a county magnate and representative of an ancient family he make it his duty to show himself before the hampshire public subscribe handsomely to the county charity call assiduously upon all the county folk and lay himself out in a word to take that position in hampshire and in the empire afterwards to which he think his prodigious talent justly entitle he
lady jane be instruct to be friendly with the fuddlestones and the wapshot and the other famous baronet their neighbour
their carriage might frequently be see in the queen s crawley avenue now they din pretty frequently at the hall where the cookery be so good that it be clear lady jane very seldom have a hand in it and in return pitt and his wife most energetically din out in all sort of weather and at all sort of distance
for though pitt do not care for joviality be a frigid man of poor hearth and appetite yet he consider that to be hospitable and condescending be quite incumbent on-his station and every time that he get a headache from too long an after-dinner sitting he feel that he be a martyr to duty
he talk about crop corn-law politic with the good country gentleman
he who have be formerly inclined to be a sad free-thinker on these point enter into poaching and game preserve with ardour
he donot hunt he benot a hunt man he be a man of book and peaceful habit but he think that the breed of horse must be keep up in the country and that the breed of fox must therefore be look to and for his part if his friend sir huddlestone fuddlestone like to draw his country and meet as of old the f
hound use to do at queen s crawley he should be happy to see he there and the gentleman of the fuddlestone hunt

many young gentleman canter up on thoroughbred hack spatter-dash to the knee and enter the house to drink cherry-brandy and pay their respect to the lady or more modest and sportsmanlike divest themselves of their mud-boot exchange their hack for their hunter and warm their blood by a preliminary gallop round the lawn

the reverend bute crawley who have be too modest to appear at the public meet before his nephew s window whom tom moody remember forty year back a slender divine rid the wild horse jump the wide brook and lark over the new gate in the country--his reverence we say happen to trot out from the rectory lane on his powerful black horse just as sir huddlestone pass he join the worthy baronet

chapter xlvi struggles and trials our friend at brompton be meanwhile pass their christmas after their fashion and in a manner by no mean too cheerful

but amelia have never forgive that smith to this day though he be now a peaceful apothecary near leicester square

chapter xlvii gaunt house all the world know that lord steyne s town palace stand in gaunt square out of which great gaunt street lead whither we first conduct rebecca in the time of the depart sir pitt crawley
peer over the railing and through the black tree into the garden of the square you see a few miserable governess with wan-face pupil wander round and round it and round the dreary grass-plot in the centre of which rise the statue of lord gaunt who fight at minden in a three-tail wig and otherwise habit like a roman emperor
gaunt house occupy nearly a side of the square
the remain three side be compose of mansion that have pass away into dowagerism--tall dark house with window-frame of stone or pick out of a light red
little light seem to be behind those lean comfortless casement now and hospitality to have pass away from those door as much as the laced lacquey and link-boy of old time who use to put out their torch in the blank iron extinguisher that still flank the lamp over the step
brass plate have penetrate into the square--doctor the diddlesex bank western branch--the english and european reunion c it have a dreary look--nor be my lord steyne s palace less dreary
all i have ever see of it be the vast wall in front with the rustic column at the great gate through which an old porter peer sometimes with a fat and gloomy red face--and over the wall the garret and bedroom window and the chimney out of which there seldom come any smoke now
for the present lord steyne live at naples prefer the view of the bay and capri and vesuvius to the dreary aspect of the wall in gaunt square
a few score yard down new gaunt street and lead into gaunt mews indeed be a little modest back door which you would not remark from that of any of the other stable
but many a little close carriage have stop at that door as my informant little tom eaves who know everything and who show i the place tell i

it conduct to the famous petit appartement of lord steyne--one sir fit up all in ivory and white satin another in ebony and black velvet there be a little banqueting-room take from sallust s house at pompeii and paint by cosway--a little private kitchen in which every saucepan be silver and all the spit be gold
it be there that egalite orleans roasted partridge on the night when he and the marquis of steyne win a hundred thousand from a great personage at ombre
half of the money go to the french revolution half to purchase lord gaunt s marquisate and garter--and the remainder-- but it form no part of our scheme to tell what become of the remainder for every shilling of which and a great deal more little tom eaves who know everybody s affair be ready to account
besides his town palace the marquis have castle and palace in various quarter of the three kingdom whereof the description may be find in the road-book--castle strongbow with its wood on the shannon shore gaunt castle in carmarthenshire where richard ii be take prisoner--gauntly hall in yorkshire where i have be inform there be two hundred silver teapot for the breakfast of the guest of the house with everything to correspond in splendour and stillbrook in hampshire which be my lord s farm an humble place of residence of which we all remember the wonderful furniture which be sell at my lord s demise by a late celebrate auctioneer
the marchioness of steyne be of the renowned and ancient family of the caerlyons marquises of camelot who have preserve the old faith ever since the conversion of the venerable druid their first ancestor and whose pedigree go far beyond the date of the arrival of king brute in these island
pendragon be the title of the eld son of the house
the son have be call arthur uthers and caradocs from immemorial time
their head have fall in many a loyal conspiracy
elizabeth chop off the head of the arthur of her day who have be chamberlain to philip and mary and carry letter between the queen of scot and her uncle the guises
a cadet of the house be an officer of the great duke and distinguish in the famous saint bartholomew conspiracy
during the whole of mary s confinement the house of camelot conspire in her behalf
it be as much injure by its charge in fit out an armament against the spaniards during the time of the armada as by the fine and confiscation levy on it by elizabeth for harbouring of priest obstinate recusancy and popish misdoing
a recreant of james s time be momentarily pervert from his religion by the argument of that great theologian and the fortune of the family somewhat restore by his timely weakness
but the earl of camelot of the reign of charles return to the old creed of his family and they continue to fight for it and ruin themselves for it as long as there be a stuart leave to head or to instigate a rebellion
lady mary caerlyon be bring up at a parisian convent the dauphiness marie antoinette be her godmother

this dark presentiment also haunt lord steyne

that be a vanity but may every man who read this have a wholesome portion of it through life i beg aye though my reader be five hundred thousand
sit down gentleman and fall to with a good hearty appetite the fat the lean the gravy the horse-radish as you like it--don t spare it
another glass of wine jones my boy--a little bit of the sunday side
yes let we eat our fill of the vain thing and be thankful therefor
and let we make the good of becky s aristocratic pleasure likewise--for these too like all other mortal delight be but transitory

if every person be to be banish from society who run into debt and cannot pay--if we be to be peer into everybody s private life speculate upon their income and cut they if we donot approve of their expenditure--why what a howl wilderness and intolerable dwell vanity fair would be
every man s hand would be against his neighbour in this case my dear sir and the benefit of civilization would be do away with
we should be quarrel abuse avoid one another

nowhere in the race

chapter lii in which lord steyne show himself in a most amiable light when lord steyne be benevolently dispose he do nothing by half and his kindness towards the crawley family do the great honour to his benevolent discrimination
his lordship extend his good-will to little rawdon he point out to the boy s parent the necessity of send he to a public school that he be of an age now when emulation the first principle of the latin language pugilistic exercise and the society of his fellow-boy would be of the great benefit to the boy
his father object that he be not rich enough to send the child to a good public school his mother that briggs be a capital mistress for he and have bring he on as indeed be the fact famously in english the latin rudiment and in general learning but all these objection disappear before the generous perseverance of the marquis of steyne
his lordship be one of the governor of that famous old collegiate institution call the whitefriars
it have be a cistercian convent in old day when the smithfield which be contiguous to it be a tournament ground
obstinate heretic use to be bring thither convenient for burn hard by
henry viii the defender of the faith seize upon the monastery and its possession and hang and torture some of the monk who could not accommodate themselves to the pace of his reform
finally a great merchant buy the house and land adjoining in which and with the help of other wealthy endowment of land and money he establish a famous foundation hospital for old man and child
an extern school grow round the old almost monastic foundation which subsist still with its middle-age costume and usage--and all cistercians pray that it may long flourish
of this famous house some of the great nobleman prelate and dignitary in england be governor and as the boy be very comfortably lodge fed and educate and subsequently induct to good scholarship at the university and living in the church many little gentleman be devoted to the ecclesiastical profession from their tender year and there be considerable emulation to procure nomination for the foundation
it be originally intend for the son of poor and deserve cleric and laic but many of the noble governor of the institution with an enlarged and rather capricious benevolence select all sort of object for their bounty
to get an education for nothing and a future livelihood and profession assure be so excellent a scheme that some of the rich people do not disdain it and not only great man s relation but great man themselves send their son to profit by the chance--right rev
prelate send their own kinsman or the son of their clergy while on the other hand some great nobleman do not disdain to patronize the child of their confidential servant--so that a lad enter this establishment have every variety of youthful society wherewith to mingle
rawdon crawley though the only book which he study be the racing calendar and though his chief recollection of polite learning be connect with the flogging which he receive at eton in his early youth have that decent and honest reverence for classical learning which all english gentleman feel and be glad to think that his son be to have a provision for life perhaps and a certain opportunity of become a scholar
and although his boy be his chief solace and companion and endear to he by a thousand small tie about which he do not care to speak to his wife who have all along show the utmost indifference to their son yet rawdon agree at once to part with he and to give up his own great comfort and benefit for the sake of the welfare of the little lad
he do not know how fond he be of the child until it become necessary to let he go away
when he be go he feel more sad and downcast than he care to own--far sad than the boy himself who be happy enough to enter a new career and find companion of his own age

the elder rawdon be thankful for the fondness of mother and daughter

he have be bring up by a kind weak and tender woman who have no pride about anything but about he and whose heart be so pure and whose bearing be so meek and humble that she could not but need be a true lady
she busy herself in gentle office and quiet duty if she never say brilliant thing she never speak or think unkind one guileless and artless love and pure indeed how could our poor little amelia be other than a real gentlewoman

you who see your better bear up under this shame every day meekly suffering under the slight of fortune gentle and unpitied poor and rather despise for their poverty do you ever step down from your prosperity and wash the foot of these poor weary beggar
the very thought of they be odious and low
there must be class--there must be rich and poor dives say smack his claret it be well if he even send the broken meat out to lazarus sit under the window
very true but think how mysterious and often unaccountable it be--that lottery of life which give to this man the purple and fine linen and send to the other rag for garment and dog for comforter
so i must own that without much repining on the contrary with something akin to gratitude amelia take the crumb that her father-in-law let drop now and then and with they feed her own parent
directly she understand it to be her duty it be this young woman s nature lady she be but thirty still and we choose to call she a young woman even at that age it be i say her nature to sacrifice herself and to fle all that she have at the foot of the beloved object
during what long thankless night have she work out her finger for little georgy whilst at home with she what buffet scorn privation poverty have she endure for father and mother
and in the midst of all these solitary resignation and unseen sacrifice she do not respect herself any more than the world respect she but i believe thought in her heart that she be a poor-spirit despicable little creature whose luck in life be only too good for her merit
o you poor woman
o you poor secret martyr and victim whose life be a torture who be stretch on rack in your bedroom and who lie your head down on the block daily at the drawing-room table every man who watch your pain or peer into those dark place where the torture be administer to you must pity you--and--and thank god that he have a beard
i recollect see year ago at the prison for idiot and madman at bicetre near paris a poor wretch bend down under the bondage of his imprisonment and his personal infirmity to whom one of our party give a halfpenny worth of snuff in a cornet or screw of paper
the kindness be too much for the poor epileptic creature
he cry in an anguish of delight and gratitude if anybody give you and i a thousand a year or save our life we could not be so affected
and so if you properly tyrannize over a woman you will find a h p orth of kindness act upon she and bring tear into her eye as though you be an angel benefit she
some such boon as these be the good which fortune allot to poor little amelia
her life begin not unprosperously have come down to this--to a mean prison and a long ignoble bondage
little george visit her captivity sometimes and console it with feeble gleam of encouragement
russell square be the boundary of her prison she might walk thither occasionally but be always back to sleep in her cell at night to perform cheerless duty to watch by thankless sick-bed to suffer the harassment and tyranny of querulous disappoint old age
how many thousand of people be there woman for the most part who be doom to endure this long slavery who be hospital nurse without wage--sister of charity if you like without the romance and the sentiment of sacrifice--who strive fast watch and suffer unpitied and fade away ignobly and unknown
the hidden and awful wisdom which apportion the destiny of mankind be pleased so to humiliate and cast down the tender good and wise and to set up the selfish the foolish or the wicked
oh be humble my brother in your prosperity
be gentle with those who be less lucky if not more deserving
think what right have you to be scornful whose virtue be a deficiency of temptation whose success may be a chance whose rank may be an ancestor s accident whose prosperity be very likely a satire
they bury amelia s mother in the churchyard at brompton upon just such a rainy dark day as amelia recollect when first she have be there to marry george
her little boy sit by her side in pompous new sable

she slave toil patch and mend sing and play backgammon read out the newspaper cook dish for old sedley walk he out sedulously into kensington gardens or the brompton lanes listen to his story with untiring smile and affectionate hypocrisy or sit musing by his side and commune with her own thought and reminiscence as the old man feeble and querulous sun himself on the garden bench and prattle about his wrong or his sorrow

she be such a miserable wicked sinner

i know that the account of this kind of solitary imprisonment be insufferably tedious unless there be some cheerful or humorous incident to enliven it--a tender gaoler for instance or a waggish commandant of the fortress or a mouse to come out and play about latude s beard and whisker or a subterranean passage under the castle dig by trenck with his nail and a toothpick the historian have no such enliven incident to relate in the narrative of amelia s captivity
fancy she if you please during this period very sad but always ready to smile when speak to in a very mean poor not to say vulgar position of life sing song make pudding play card mend stocking for her old father s benefit
so never mind whether she be a heroine or no or you and i however old scold and bankrupt--may we have in our last day a kind soft shoulder on which to lean and a gentle hand to soothe our gouty old pillow
old sedley grow very fond of his daughter after his wife s death and amelia have her consolation in do her duty by the old man
but we be not go to leave these two people long in such a low and ungenteel station of life
well day as far as worldly prosperity go be in store for both

major dobbin have easily succeed in get leave from his good-natured commandant to proceed to madras and thence probably to europe on urgent private affair never cease travel night and day until he reach his journey s end and have direct his march with such celerity that he arrive at madras in a high fever
his servant who accompany he bring he to the house of the friend with whom he have resolve to stay until his departure for europe in a state of delirium and it be think for many many day that he would never travel far than the burying-ground of the church of st
george s where the troop should fire a salvo over his grave and where many a gallant officer lie far away from his home
here as the poor fellow lie tossing in his fever the people who watch he might have hear he rave about amelia

john
he recover rally relapse again have undergo such a process of blood-letting and calomel as show the strength of his original constitution
he be almost a skeleton when they put he on board the ramchunder east indiaman captain bragg from calcutta touch at madras and so weak and prostrate that his friend who have tend he through his illness prophesy that the honest major would never survive the voyage and that he would pass some morning shroud in flag and hammock over the ship s side and carry down to the sea with he the relic that he wear at his heart
but whether it be the sea air or the hope which spring up in he afresh from the day that the ship spread her canvas and stand out of the road towards home our friend begin to amend and he be quite well though as gaunt as a greyhound before they reach the cape

he have pass ten year in bengal
constant dinner tiffin pale ale and claret the prodigious labour of cutcherry and the refreshment of brandy-pawnee which he be force to take there have their effect upon waterloo sedley

he be rather thin than when we last see he but have gain in majesty and solemnity of demeanour
he have resume the mustachio to which his service at waterloo entitle he and swagger about on deck in a magnificent velvet cap with a gold band and a profuse ornamentation of pin and jewellery about his person
he take breakfast in his cabin and dress as solemnly to appear on the quarter-deck as if he be go to turn out for bond street or the course at calcutta
he bring a native servant with he who be his valet and pipe-bearer and who wear the sedley crest in silver on his turban
that oriental menial have a wretched life under the tyranny of jos sedley
jos be as vain of his person as a woman and take as long a time at his toilette as any fading beauty
the youngster among the passenger young chaffers of the 150 th and poor little rickett come home after his third fever use to draw out sedley at the cuddy-table and make he tell prodigious story about himself and his exploit against tiger and napoleon

he disappear rather in a panic during a two-day gale in which he have the porthole of his cabin batten down and remain in his cot read the washerwoman of finchley common leave on board the ramchunder by the right honourable the lady emily hornblower wife of the rev

although the sight of that magnificent round of beef and the silver tankard suggestive of real british home-brew ale and porter which perennially greet the eye of the traveller return from foreign part who enter the coffee-room of the george be so invigorating and delightful that a man enter such a comfortable snug homely english inn might well like to stop some day there yet dobbin begin to talk about a post-chaise instantly and be no soon at southampton than he wish to be on the road to london

this great effort of genius which be still in the possession of george s mother be as follow on selfishness--of all the vice which degrade the human character selfishness be the most odious and contemptible
an undue love of self lead to the most monstrous crime and occasion the great misfortune both in states and families
as a selfish man will impoverish his family and often bring they to ruin so a selfish king bring ruin on his people and often plunge they into war
example the selfishness of achilles as remark by the poet homer occasion a thousand woe to the greeks--muri achaiois alge etheke-- hom
il
a
2
the selfishness of the late napoleon bonaparte occasion innumerable war in europe and cause he to perish himself in a miserable island--that of saint helena in the atlantic ocean
we see by these example that we be not to consult our own interest and ambition but that we be to consider the interest of other as well as our own

chapter lix the old piano the major s visit leave old john sedley in a great state of agitation and excitement
his daughter could not induce he to settle down to his customary occupation or amusement that night
he pass the evening fumbling amongst his box and desk untie his paper with tremble hand and sort and arrange they against jos s arrival
he have they in the great order--his tape and his file his receipt and his letter with lawyer and correspondent the document relative to the wine project which fail from a most unaccountable accident after commence with the most splendid prospect the coal project which only a want of capital prevent from become the most successful scheme ever put before the public the patent saw-mill and sawdust consolidation project c c
all night until a very late hour he pass in the preparation of these document tremble about from one room to another with a quivering candle and shaky hand

there be some splendid tailor shop in the high street of southampton in the fine plate-glass window of which hang gorgeous waistcoat of all sort of silk and velvet and gold and crimson and picture of the last new fashion in which those wonderful gentleman with quiz glass and hold on to little boy with the exceed large eye and curly hair ogle lady in rid habit prance by the statue of achilles at apsley house

for jos s former shyness and blundering blushing timidity have give way to a more candid and courageous self-assertion of his worth
i donot care about own it waterloo sedley would say to his friend i be a dressy man and though rather uneasy if the lady look at he at the government house ball and though he blush and turn away alarmed under their glance it be chiefly from a dread lest they should make love to he that he avoid they be averse to marriage altogether
but there be no such swell in calcutta as waterloo sedley i have hear say and he have the handsome turn-out give the good bachelor dinner and have the fine plate in the whole place

at length he drive leisurely to london on the third day and in the new waistcoat the native with chatter tooth shudder in a shawl on the box by the side of the new european servant jos puff his pipe at interval within and look so majestic that the little boy cry hooray and many people think he must be a governor-general
he i promise do not decline the obsequious invitation of the landlord to alight and refresh himself in the neat country town

at farnham he stop to view the bishop s castle and to partake of a light dinner of stew eel veal cutlet and french bean with a bottle of claret
he be cold over bagshot heath where the native chatter more and more and jos sahib take some brandy-and-water in fact when he drive into town he be as full of wine beer meat pickle cherry-brandy and tobacco as the steward s cabin of a steam-packet

that second-floor arch in a london house look up and down the well of the staircase and command the main thoroughfare by which the inhabitant be pass by which cook lurk down before daylight to scour her pot and pan in the kitchen by which young master stealthily ascend have leave his boot in the hall and let himself in after dawn from a jolly night at the club down which miss come rustle in fresh ribbon and spread muslin brilliant and beautiful and prepare for conquest and the ball or master tommy slide prefer the banister for a mode of conveyance and disdain danger and the stair down which the mother be fondly carry smile in her strong husband s arm as he step steadily step by step and follow by the monthly nurse on the day when the medical man have pronounce that the charming patient may go downstairs up which john lurk to bed yawning with a sputtering tallow candle and to gather up before sunrise the boot which be await he in the passage--that stair up or down which baby be carry old people be help guest be marshal to the ball the parson walk to the christening the doctor to the sick-room and the undertaker s man to the upper floor--what a memento of life death and vanity it be--that arch and stair--if you choose to consider it and sit on the landing look up and down the well

which of the dead be most tenderly and passionately deplore
those who love the survivor the least i believe
the death of a child occasion a passion of grief and frantic tear such as your end brother reader will never inspire
the death of an infant which scarce know you which a week s absence from you would have cause to forget you will strike you down more than the loss of your close friend or your first-bear son--a man grow like yourself with child of his own
we may be harsh and stern with judah and simeon--our love and pity gush out for benjamin the little one

that must be a strange feeling when a day of our life come and we say to-morrow success or failure willnot matter much and the sun will rise and all the myriad of mankind go to their work or their pleasure as usual but i shall be out of the turmoil so there come one morning and sunrise when all the world get up and set about its various work and pleasure with the exception of old john sedley who be not to fight with fortune or to hope or scheme any more but to go and take up a quiet and utterly unknown residence in a churchyard at brompton by the side of his old wife

yes i think that will be the well ending of the two after all
suppose you be particularly rich and well-to-do and say on that last day i be very rich i be tolerably well know i have live all my life in the good society and thank heaven come of a most respectable family
i have serve my king and country with honour
i be in parliament for several year where i may say my speech be listen to and pretty well receive

i leave my daughter with ten thousand pound apiece--very good portion for girl i bequeath my plate and furniture my house in baker street with a handsome jointure to my widow for her life and my land property besides money in the fund and my cellar of well-select wine in baker street to my son

i be not endow either with brain or with good fortune and confess that i have commit a hundred mistake and blunder

i cannot pay what i owe

pumpernickel stand in the midst of a happy valley through which sparkle--to mingle with the rhine somewhere but i have not the map at hand to say exactly at what point--the fertilize stream of the pump
in some place the river be big enough to support a ferry-boat in other to turn a mill in pumpernickel itself the last transparency but three the great and renowned victor aurelius xiv build a magnificent bridge on which his own statue rise surround by water-nymph and emblem of victory peace and plenty he have his foot on the neck of a prostrate turk--history say he engage and run a janissary through the body at the relief of vienna by sobieski--but quite undisturbed by the agony of that prostrate mahometan who writhe at his foot in the most ghastly manner the prince smile blandly and point with his truncheon in the direction of the aurelius platz where he begin to erect a new palace that would have be the wonder of his age have the great-souled prince but have fund to complete it
but the completion of monplaisir monblaisir the honest german folk call it be stop for lack of ready money and it and its park and garden be now in rather a faded condition and not more than ten time big enough to accommodate the court of the reigning sovereign
the garden be arrange to emulate those of versailles and amidst the terrace and grove there be some huge allegorical waterworks still which spout and froth stupendously upon fete-day and frighten one with their enormous aquatic insurrection
there be the trophonius cave in which by some artifice the leaden tritons be make not only to spout water but to play the most dreadful groan out of their lead conchs--there be the nymphbath and the niagara cataract which the people of the neighbourhood admire beyond expression when they come to the yearly fair at the opening of the chamber or to the fete with which the happy little nation still celebrate the birthday and marriage-day of its princely governor
then from all the town of the duchy which stretch for nearly ten mile--from bolkum which lie on its western frontier bidding defiance to prussia from grogwitz where the prince have a hunting-lodge and where his dominion be separate by the pump river from those of the neighbour prince of potzenthal from all the little village which besides these three great city dot over the happy principality--from the farm and the mill along the pump come troop of people in red petticoat and velvet head-dress or with three-cornered hat and pipe in their mouth who flock to the residenz and share in the pleasure of the fair and the festivity there
then the theatre be open for nothing then the water of monblaisir begin to play it be lucky that there be company to behold they for one would be afraid to see they alone then there come mountebank and rid troop the way in which his transparency be fascinate by one of the horse-rider be well know and it be believe that la petite vivandiere as she be call be a spy in the french interest and the delighted people be permit to march through room after room of the grand ducal palace and admire the slippery floor the rich hanging and the spittoon at the door of all the innumerable chamber
there be one pavilion at monblaisir which aurelius victor xv have arrange--a great prince but too fond of pleasure--and which i be tell be a perfect wonder of licentious elegance
it be paint with the story of bacchus and ariadne and the table work in and out of the room by mean of a windlass so that the company be serve without any intervention of domestic
but the place be shut up by barbara aurelius xv s widow a severe and devout princess of the house of bolkum and regent of the duchy during her son s glorious minority and after the death of her husband cut off in the pride of his pleasure
the theatre of pumpernickel be know and famous in that quarter of germany
it languish a little when the present duke in his youth insist upon have his own opera play there and it be say one day in a fury from his place in the orchestra when he attend a rehearsal break a bassoon on the head of the chapel master who be conduct and lead too slow and during which time the duchess sophia write domestic comedy which must have be very dreary to witness
but the prince execute his music in private now and the duchess only give away her play to the foreigner of distinction who visit she kind little court
it be conduct with no small comfort and splendour
when there be ball though there may be four hundred people at supper there be a servant in scarlet and lace to attend upon every four and every one be serve on silver
there be festival and entertainment go continually on and the duke have his chamberlain and equerry and the duchess her mistress of the wardrobe and lady of honour just like any other and more potent potentate
the constitution be or be a moderate despotism temper by a chamber that might or might not be elect
i never certainly could hear of its sitting in my time at pumpernickel
the prime minister have lodging in a second floor and the foreign secretary occupy the comfortable lodging over zwieback s conditorey
the army consist of a magnificent band that also do duty on the stage where it be quite pleasant to see the worthy fellow march in turkish dress with rouge on and wooden scimitar or as roman warrior with ophicleide and trombone--to see they again i say at night after one have listen to they all the morning in the aurelius platz where they perform opposite the cafe where we breakfast
besides the band there be a rich and numerous staff of officer and i believe a few man

that there be feud in the place no one can deny
politic run very high at pumpernickel and party be very bitter
there be the strumpff faction and the lederlung party the one support by our envoy and the other by the french charge d affaires m
de macabau
indeed it suffice for our minister to stand up for madame strumpff who be clearly the great singer of the two and have three more note in her voice than madame lederlung her rival--it suffice i say for our minister to advance any opinion to have it instantly contradict by the french diplomatist
everybody in the town be range in one or other of these faction
the lederlung be a prettyish little creature certainly and her voice what there be of it be very sweet and there be no doubt that the strumpff be not in her first youth and beauty and certainly too stout when she come on in the last scene of the sonnambula for instance in her night-chemise with a lamp in her hand and have to go out of the window and pass over the plank of the mill it be all she could do to squeeze out of the window and the plank use to bend and creak again under her weight--but how she pour out the finale of the opera

whereas the little lederlung--but a truce to this gossip--the fact be that these two woman be the two flag of the french and the english party at pumpernickel and the society be divide in its allegiance to those two great nation
we have on our side the home minister the master of the horse the duke s private secretary and the prince s tutor whereas of the french party be the foreign minister the commander-in-chief s lady who have serve under napoleon and the hof-marschall and his wife who be glad enough to get the fashion from pans and always have they and her cap by m

neither tapeworm nor macabau ever send home a dispatch to his government without a most savage series of attack upon his rival
for instance on our side we would write the interest of great britain in this place and throughout the whole of germany be perille by the continuance in office of the present french envoy this man be of a character so infamous that he will stick at no falsehood or hesitate at no crime to attain his end
he poison the mind of the court against the english minister represent the conduct of great britain in the most odious and atrocious light and be unhappily back by a minister whose ignorance and necessity be as notorious as his influence be fatal on their side they would say m
de tapeworm continue his system of stupid insular arrogance and vulgar falsehood against the great nation in the world
yesterday he be hear to speak lightly of her royal highness madame the duchess of berri on a former occasion he insult the heroic duke of angouleme and dare to insinuate that h r h
the duke of orleans be conspire against the august throne of the lily

when one side or the other have write any particularly spicy dispatch news of it be sure to slip out
before the winter be far advanced it be actually on record that emmy take a night and receive company with great propriety and modesty

chapter lxiv a vagabond chapter we must pass over a part of mrs
rebecca crawley s biography with that lightness and delicacy which the world demand--the moral world that have perhaps no particular objection to vice but an insuperable repugnance to hear vice call by its proper name
there be thing we do and know perfectly well in vanity fair though we never speak of they as the ahrimanians worship the devil but donot mention he and a polite public will no more bear to read an authentic description of vice than a truly refined english or american female will permit the word breeche to be pronounce in her chaste hearing
and yet madam both be walk the world before our face every day without much shock we
if you be to blush every time they go by what complexion you would have
it be only when their naughty name be call out that your modesty have any occasion to show alarm or sense of outrage and it have be the wish of the present writer all through this story deferentially to submit to the fashion at present prevailing and only to hint at the existence of wickedness in a light easy and agreeable manner so that nobody s fine feeling may be offend
i defy any one to say that our becky who have certainly some vice have not be present to the public in a perfectly genteel and inoffensive manner
in describe this siren sing and smile coax and cajoling the author with modest pride ask his reader all round have he once forget the law of politeness and show the monster s hideous tail above water
no
those who like may peep down under wave that be pretty transparent and see it writhe and twirling diabolically hideous and slimy flap amongst bone or curl round corps but above the waterline i ask have not everything be proper agreeable and decorous and have any the most squeamish immoralist in vanity fair a right to cry fie
when however the siren disappear and dive below down among the dead man the water of course grow turbid over she and it be labour lose to look into it ever so curiously
they look pretty enough when they sit upon a rock twang their harp and comb their hair and sing and beckon to you to come and hold the look-glass but when they sink into their native element depend on it those mermaid be about no good and we have well not examine the fiendish marine cannibal revel and feast on their wretched pickle victim
and so when becky be out of the way be sure that she be not particularly well employ and that the less that be say about her doing be in fact the well
if we be to give a full account of her proceeding during a couple of year that follow after the curzon street catastrophe there might be some reason for people to say this book be improper
the action of very vain heartless pleasure-seek people be very often improper as be many of your my friend with the grave face and spotless reputation--but that be merely by the way and what be those of a woman without faith--or love--or character
and i be inclined to think that there be a period in mrs becky s life when she be seize not by remorse but by a kind of despair and absolutely neglect her person and do not even care for her reputation
this abattement and degradation do not take place all at once it be bring about by degree after her calamity and after many struggle to keep up--as a man who go overboard hang on to a spar whilst any hope be leave and then fling it away and go down when he find that struggle be in vain
she linger about london whilst her husband be make preparation for his departure to his seat of government and it be believe make more than one attempt to see her brother-in-law sir pitt crawley and to work upon his feeling which she have almost enlist in her favour

that young gentleman be consign to the entire guardianship of his aunt and uncle the former of whom have always possess a great share of the child s affection
his mamma write he a neat letter from boulogne when she quit england in which she request he to mind his book and say she be go to take a continental tour during which she would have the pleasure of write to he again
but she never do for a year afterwards and not indeed until sir pitt s only boy always sickly die of hooping-cough and measle--then rawdon s mamma write the most affectionate composition to her darling son who be make heir of queen s crawley by this accident and draw more closely than ever to the kind lady whose tender heart have already adopt he

our darling becky s first flight be not very far
she perch upon the french coast at boulogne that refuge of so much exiled english innocence and there live in rather a genteel widow manner with a femme de chambre and a couple of room at an hotel
she din at the table d hote where people think her very pleasant and where she entertain her neighbour by story of her brother sir pitt and her great london acquaintance talk that easy fashionable slip-slop which have so much effect upon certain folk of small breeding

becky be very respectable and orderly at first but the life of humdrum virtue grow utterly tedious to she before long
it be the same routine every day the same dulness and comfort the same drive over the same stupid bois de boulogne the same company of an evening the same blair s sermon of a sunday night--the same opera always be act over and over again becky be die of weariness when luckily for she young mr

then she determine upon a boarding-house existence and live for some time at that famous mansion keep by madame de saint amour in the rue royale at paris where she begin exercise her grace and fascination upon the shabby dandy and fly-blow beauty who frequent her landlady s salon
becky love society and indeed could no more exist without it than an opium-eater without his dram and she be happy enough at the period of her boarding-house life

here as at paris becky be a boarding-house queen and rule in select pension

so our little wanderer go about set up her tent in various city of europe as restless as ulysses or bampfylde moore carew
her taste for disrespectability grow more and more remarkable
she become a perfect bohemian ere long herd with people whom it would make your hair stand on end to meet

hemp the officer read out periodically at the sheriffs court--young gentleman of very good family often only that the latter disown they frequenter of billiard-room and estaminet patron of foreign race and gaming-table
they people the debtor prison--they drink and swagger--they fight and brawl--they run away without pay--they have duel with french and german officer--they cheat mr
spooney at ecarte--they get the money and drive off to baden in magnificent britzkas--they try their infallible martingale and lurk about the table with empty pocket shabby bully penniless buck until they can swindle a jew banker with a sham bill of exchange or find another mr
spooney to rob
the alternation of splendour and misery which these people undergo be very queer to view
their life must be one of great excitement

all the poor all the humble all honest folk all good man who know he love that kind-hearted and simple gentleman
as for emmy have she not do her duty
she have her picture of george for a consolation
chapter lxvii which contain births marriages and death whatever becky s private plan might be by which dobbin s true love be to be crown with success the little woman think that the secret might keep and indeed be by no mean so much interested about anybody s welfare as about her own she have a great number of thing pertain to herself to consider and which concern she a great deal more than major dobbin s happiness in this life
she find herself suddenly and unexpectedly in snug comfortable quarter surround by friend kindness and good-natured simple people such as she have not meet with for many a long day and wanderer as she be by force and inclination there be moment when rest be pleasant to she
as the most harden arab that ever career across the desert over the hump of a dromedary like to repose sometimes under the date-tree by the water or to come into the city walk into the bazaar refresh himself in the bath and say his prayer in the mosque before he go out again maraud so jos s tent and pilau be pleasant to this little ishmaelite
she picket her steed hung up her weapon and warm herself comfortably by his fire
the halt in that roving restless life be inexpressibly soothing and pleasant to she
so please herself she try with all her might to please everybody and we know that she be eminent and successful as a practitioner in the art of give pleasure

osborne s charming friend and though she do not ask to go to court yet the most august and transparent personages there hear of her fascination and be quite curious to know she

love and liberty be interpret by those simple germans in a way which honest folk in yorkshire and somersetshire little understand and a lady might in some philosophic and civilized town be divorce ever so many time from her respective husband and keep her character in society

gravesend june 20 the ramchunder east indiaman come into the river this morning have on board 14 officer and 132 rank and file of this gallant corps
they have be absent from england fourteen year have be embark the year after waterloo in which glorious conflict they take an active part and have subsequently distinguish themselves in the burmese war
the veteran colonel sir michael o dowd k c b with his lady and sister land here yesterday with captains posky stubble macraw malony lieutenants smith jones thompson f
thomson ensigns hicks and grady the band on the pier play the national anthem and the crowd loudly cheer the gallant veteran as they go into wayte s hotel where a sumptuous banquet be provide for the defender of old england
during the repast which we need not say be serve up in wayte s good style the cheering continue so enthusiastically that lady o dowd and the colonel come forward to the balcony and drank the health of their fellow-countryman in a bumper of wayte s good claret on a second occasion jos read a brief announcement--major dobbin have join the th regiment at chatham and subsequently he promulgate account of the presentation at the drawing-room of colonel sir michael o dowd k c b lady o dowd by mrs

major posky she take he on the death of his first wife have resolve never to marry out of the regiment
lady o dowd be also so attached to it that she say if anything be to happen to mick bedad shewould come back and marry some of them
but the major-general be quite well and live in great splendour at o dowdstown with a pack of beagle and with the exception of perhaps their neighbour hoggarty of castle hoggarty he be the first man of his county
her ladyship still dance jig and insist on stand up with the master of the horse at the lord lieutenant s last ball
both she and glorvina declare that dobbin have use the latter sheamfully but posky fall in glorvina be console and a beautiful turban from paris appease the wrath of lady o dowd
when colonel dobbin quit the service which he do immediately after his marriage he rent a pretty little country place in hampshire not far from queen s crawley where after the passing of the reform bill sir pitt and his family constantly reside now
all idea of a peerage be out of the question the baronet s two seat in parliament be lose
he be both out of pocket and out of spirit by that catastrophe fail in his health and prophesy the speedy ruin of the empire
lady jane and mrs
dobbin become great friend--there be a perpetual crossing of pony-chaise between the hall and the evergreens the colonel s place rent of his friend major ponto who be abroad with his family
her ladyship be godmother to mrs
dobbin s child which bear her name and be christen by the rev
james crawley who succeed his father in the living and a pretty close friendship subsist between the two lad george and rawdon who hunt and shoot together in the vacation be both enter of the same college at cambridge and quarrel with each other about lady jane s daughter with whom they be both of course in love
a match between george and that young lady be long a favourite scheme of both the matron though i have hear that miss crawley herself incline towards her cousin
mrs
rawdon crawley s name be never mention by either family
there be reason why all should be silent regard she
for wherever mr
joseph sedley go she travel likewise and that infatuated man seem to be entirely her slave
the colonel s lawyer inform he that his brother-in-law have effect a heavy insurance upon his life whence it be probable that he have be raise money to discharge debt
he procure prolonged leave of absence from the east india house and indeed his infirmity be daily increase
on hear the news about the insurance amelia in a good deal of alarm entreat her husband to go to brussels where jos then be and inquire into the state of his affair
the colonel quit home with reluctance for he be deeply immerse in his history of the punjaub which still occupy he and much alarmed about his little daughter whom he idolize and who be just recover from the chicken-pox and go to brussels and find jos live at one of the enormous hotel in that city
mrs
crawley who have her carriage give entertainment and live in a very genteel manner occupy another suite of apartment in the same hotel
the colonel of course do not desire to see that lady or even think proper to notify his arrival at brussels except privately to jos by a message through his valet
jos beg the colonel to come and see he that night when mrs
crawley would be at a soiree and when they could meet alone
he find his brother-in-law in a condition of pitiable infirmity--and dreadfully afraid of rebecca though eager in his praise of she
she tend he through a series of unheard-of illness with a fidelity most admirable
she have be a daughter to he
but--but--oh for god s sake do come and live near i and--and--see i sometimes whimper out the unfortunate man
the colonel s brow darken at this
we cannot jos he say
consider the circumstance amelia cannot visit you i swear to you--i swear to you on the bible gasp out joseph want to kiss the book that she be as innocent as a child as spotless as your own wife it may be so say the colonel gloomily but emmy cannot come to you
be a man jos break off this disreputable connection
come home to your family
we hear your affair be involve involve cry jos
who have tell such calumny
all my money be place out most advantageously
mrs
crawley--that be--i mean--it be lay out to the good interest you be not in debt then
why do you insure your life i think--a little present to she--in case anything happen and you know my health be so delicate--common gratitude you know--and i intend to leave all my money to you--and i can spare it out of my income indeed i can cry out william s weak brother-in-law
the colonel beseech jos to fly at once--to go back to india whither mrs
crawley could not follow he to do anything to break off a connection which might have the most fatal consequence to he
jos clasp his hand and cry he would go back to india
he would do anything only he must have time they mustnot say anything to mrs
crawley--she d--she d kill i if she know it
you donot know what a terrible woman she be the poor wretch say
then why not come away with i say dobbin in reply but jos have not the courage
he would see dobbin again in the morning he must on no account say that he have be there
he must go now
becky might come in and dobbin quit he full of foreboding
he never see jos more
three month afterwards joseph sedley die at aix-la-chapelle
it be find that all his property have be muddle away in speculation and be represent by valueless share in different bubble company
all his available asset be the two thousand pound for which his life be insure and which be leave equally between his beloved sister amelia wife of c and his friend and invaluable attendant during sickness rebecca wife of lieutenant-colonel rawdon crawley c b who be appoint administratrix
the solicitor of the insurance company swear it be the black case that ever have come before he talk of send a commission to aix to examine into the death and the company refuse payment of the policy
but mrs or lady crawley as she style herself come to town at once attend with her solicitor messrs
burke thurtell and hayes of thavies inn and dare the company to refuse the payment
they invite examination they declare that she be the object of an infamous conspiracy which have be pursue she all through life and triumph finally
the money be pay and her character establish but colonel dobbin send back his share of the legacy to the insurance office and rigidly decline to hold any communication with rebecca
she never be lady crawley though she continue so to call herself
his excellency colonel rawdon crawley die of yellow fever at coventry island most deeply beloved and deplore and six week before the demise of his brother sir pitt
the estate consequently devolve upon the present sir rawdon crawley bart
he too have decline to see his mother to whom he make a liberal allowance and who besides appear to be very wealthy
the baronet live entirely at queen s crawley with lady jane and her daughter whilst rebecca lady crawley chiefly hang about bath and cheltenham where a very strong party of excellent people consider she to be a most injure woman
she have her enemy
who have not
her life be her answer to they
she busy herself in work of piety
she go to church and never without a footman
her name be in all the charity lists
the destitute orange-girl the neglect washerwoman the distressed muffin-man find in she a fast and generous friend
she be always have stall at fancy fairs for the benefit of these hapless being
emmy her child and the colonel come to london some time back find themselves suddenly before she at one of these fair
she cast down her eye demurely and smile as they start away from she emmy scurry off on the arm of george now grow a dash young gentleman and the colonel seize up his little janey of whom he be fonder than of anything in the world--fonder even than of his history of the punjaub
fonder than he be of i emmy think with a sigh but he never say a word to amelia that be not kind and gentle or thought of a want of her that he do not try to gratify

vanitas vanitatum

which of we have his desire
